data.title: ‘My husband is in prison but BT won’t let me cancel his TNT Sports subscription’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*

Dear Ruth,

My husband was sentenced to prison in 2024. His crime was committed before I met him, and I knew nothing about it until his arrest. It has been devastating for me.

Since then, I’ve had problems cancelling the TNT Sports package on our BT account. It’s in his name, although I am a named contact and I pay the bills.

BT said that “named contacts” could not change subscriptions but that it would accept a power of attorney. My husband set up an ordinary power of attorney before he went to prison, but BT told me it would only accept a “lasting” power of attorney (LPA).

As a retired lawyer specialising in powers of attorney, I know the “ordinary” type (also known as “general”) should be adequate for any business matters, particularly as it is only needed during the time he is in prison and he is not suffering from any lack of capacity. However, BT doesn’t accept them. I made a complaint, but it was not upheld.

The only way my husband could make an LPA is for a lawyer to be authorised to go into the prison to see him and get the paperwork signed. That would be very expensive and would take many months.

Foolishly, I tried again to reach a solution with BT recently. The £22.31 monthly charge that I’m paying for a service I’m not using was going to increase by £2 a month at the end of March. I spent one hour and 45 minutes on the phone to BT, and was told the only way I could get rid of the sports package was for them to cancel my husband’s account, at a cancellation cost of almost £600, and open an account in my own name.

Very stupidly, I agreed to do this, but then realised he would lose his email account, which is very important as all his legal and business communications come to it. Prisoners can’t access email, but I am monitoring the account with his permission.

So, I tried to undo the cancellation to put everything back where it was, even though it will involve high monthly costs. The person I spoke to managed to cancel the new account in my name, and I was told my husband’s account would be reinstated.

Then, someone from BT’s “retention team” phoned asking to speak to my husband. I explained again why this wasn’t possible. The caller said he could say nothing to me because I was not a named contact on the account, even though everyone else at BT so far has confirmed that I am.

The worry about this is making me ill. I need a telephone and broadband service and the email address is vital. It is horrible having to go through the trauma of talking about a husband in prison every time I try to speak to BT. Every call is such a nightmare that I have to spend days psyching myself up to undergo the ordeal, and every time things just get worse, not better.

Please could you help me?

SJ, Gloucestershire

Dear reader,

I can only imagine the pain of seeing your husband imprisoned, and then having to repeatedly explain his absence to unsympathetic companies like BT.

Firms have previously been criticised for the way they treat relatives of deceased customers (and many have now improved their processes), but it seems that other scenarios, such as dealing with the loved ones of incarcerated customers, are still causing misery.

I hope this column highlights the importance of companies having a framework in place when these distressing situations arise, so that compassionate and practical solutions can be found swiftly.

You deliberately arranged an ordinary power of attorney for your husband. This is similar to an LPA, but crucially, it is for a temporary period. Someone might want to give a friend or family member an ordinary power of attorney to manage their affairs if, for example, they are physically injured (but still mentally sound), are abroad for a long period of time or are in prison.

An LPA, on the other hand, can be used when someone loses the mental capacity to make their own decisions. You didn’t want to create this power of attorney as it might make it difficult for your husband to resume dealing with his affairs when he’s released in a few years’ time.

You told me: “I think because LPAs now have such a high profile, staff are often not trained to recognise and deal with ordinary powers of attorney. BT has not suggested the power of attorney is invalid, only that it chooses not to deal with them.”

You refer to your actions as “foolish” and “stupid”, but you’re being too hard on yourself.

Prisoners can only call specific numbers belonging to friends and family, and not organisations like BT, so it really is up to you to cancel this TV package – and you have been battling for 18 months to make the telecoms giant listen to you. Occasionally, it suggested a solution, and understandably, you followed its advice.

I was furious with BT on your behalf, as I have been with other companies that have failed to put things right despite the customer complaining for well over a year.

(Yes, I’m talking about Barclays and the Post Office, which I detailed in a previous column. And British Gas in another.)

Thankfully, a day after I intervened, BT called you to apologise, and said it would cancel TNT Sports. You were told that a cancellation with 30 days’ notice could have been placed by yourself, and the firm would look at retraining its staff.

As a gesture of goodwill, it said it would refund six months of the sports charges. I urged BT to reconsider, given you have been trying to cancel it for longer than this. It agreed to a refund of charges for a full year and credited your account with £270. After more nagging by me, BT also sent you flowers to apologise.

A BT spokesman said: “We are very sorry that [your reader] had difficulty cancelling TNT Sports on her husband’s account. To acknowledge her experience, we have provided her with a goodwill gesture, and the complaint has been resolved.”

– Ruth

Consumer Champion Katie Morley is currently on maternity leave. Read her previous investigations here.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph’s processing of your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Warner Bros investors back £82bn Paramount takeover
data.description:
Warner Bros’ investors have backed Paramount’s takeover, bringing the $111bn (£82bn) deal to reshape Hollywood a step closer.

An initial count of votes at an investor meeting found shareholders “overwhelmingly” supported the takeover by Paramount Skydance, the Hollywood giant run by the billionaire Ellison family.

The investor support means the tie-up between two of Hollywood’s biggest studios will probably be completed by the third quarter of this year, though it still requires approval by regulators.

Investors are backing the deal despite a widespread backlash in Hollywood, with claims that it will narrow the range of films and TV programmes made and give outsized influence to Donald Trump’s allies.

Larry Ellison, the Oracle billionaire and long-time supporter of the US president, is helping to fund the deal. His son, David Ellison, runs Paramount.

It has fuelled fears that the Ellison family will have outsized influence over the US media through their control over the Hollywood studios and their media assets, including CBS News and TikTok’s US division.

More than 1,000 actors, directors and film-makers warned in an open letter earlier this month that the takeover risked driving further consolidation in “an already concentrated media landscape”, leading to “less choice for audiences” and “fewer opportunities for creators”.

Actors including Jane Fonda, Glenn Close and Ben Stiller signed the letter, which also voiced concerns that the deal “prioritises the interests of a small group of powerful stakeholders over the broader public good”. Thousands more Hollywood A-listers subsequently signed the letter, including Robert De Niro and Sofia Coppola.

David Ellison has insisted the combination of the two Hollywood studios will not lead to fewer productions or cinematic releases.

The transaction will be partly funded by RedBird Capital, a US private equity fund that mounted a takeover bid for The Telegraph before abandoning it last year. RedBird is Paramount’s second-largest shareholder.

Paramount has also secured funding from Gulf sovereign wealth funds including Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar. Paramount has insisted the Gulf states will not be able to influence the management of the combined company.

Samuel Di Piazza Jr, the chairman of Warner Bros, said: “With Paramount, we look forward to creating an exceptional combined company that will expand consumer choice and benefit the global creative talent community.”

Paramount Skydance was formed in August last year through a takeover of Paramount Global by Skydance Media, the film company started by David Ellison in 2006.

Paramount was contacted for comment.

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine live’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: Major housebuilder almost halves in value as Iran war hits profits
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest housebuilders has almost halved in value after bosses unveiled contingency measures to avoid a debt crunch.

Crest Nicholson told investors on Tuesday that it had kickstarted emergency talks with lenders to secure “temporary” relief on its banking covenants.

The announcement, which also included a profit warning the housebuilder blamed on war in Iran, led to more than £124m being wiped off its value as shares fell by as much as 45pc.

Martyn Clark, the chief executive, said Crest had been hit by the conflict in the Middle East, fuelling the prospect of a “prolonged higher interest rate environment, renewed cost pressures and a deterioration in consumer confidence”.

This is likely to hamper sales for Crest, which now expects to generate earnings before interest and tax of just £5m to £15m for the full year, dramatically below January’s guidance of at least £32m.

As a result it is seeking relief on its debts.

The builder has cut its yearly sales guidance to between 1,400 and 1,500 homes, down from as many as 1,700. Meanwhile, it expects net debt to reach up to £120m, revised up from £15m-£65m.

It also warned of mounting construction costs triggered by soaring energy prices.

Bricks, blocks and steel are among the construction materials that are heavily reliant on energy for production.

Crest Nicholson is the latest in a string of housebuilders facing mounting financial pressures caused by the Gulf conflict, as higher inflation is likely to increase borrowing costs for buyers, hurting demand.

Berkeley Group also recently sounded the alarm by halting new land purchases and scaling back investment in its building projects.

Crest warned that new enquiries had begun to fall, noting that the South has seen the sharpest drop in activity.

Separately, the company cut its land sales revenue forecast to around £40m, less than the £75m to £100m it had targeted, and said it no longer expected to make “material” profit from selling land before its financial year ends in October.

Mr Clark said the housebuilder was moving “quickly” to shore up its balance sheet, insisting that it was well-placed to weather the uncertainty.

“We are doing what needs to be done to navigate this uncertainty to best position the business to deliver the attractive medium-term opportunity,” he said.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

data.title: Mandelson’s consultancy collapsed owing £4.5m
data.description:
Lord Mandelson’s consulting firm collapsed owing £4.5m, new filings reveal.

Global Counsel owed money to HMRC, office administrators, data companies and other business advisory firms when it went into administration in February, Companies House filings show.

The business, which provided advice to high-profile clients including Shell and TikTok, collapsed after a string of clients cut ties with the company in the wake of revelations about Lord Mandelson’s links with the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Lord Mandelson denies any wrongdoing.

Global Counsel was set up by Lord Mandelson and Benjamin Wegg-Prosser after Labour lost the 2010 general election. It had offices in cities including Singapore, Washington and Doha.

The business owed £645,000 to HMRC when it entered administration, according to a report from administrators Interpath.

Global Counsel collapsed with assets of £10.7m but administrators said they were only confident of securing £2.7m of that, with uncertainty around millions of pounds in loans to subsidiaries in Qatar and Belgium.

While HMRC and other preferential creditors are likely to see their debts repaid from that sum, other creditors face losses of £4.5m, administrators warned.

Lord Mandelson stepped down as Global Counsel’s chairman in 2024, after he was appointed as Britain’s ambassador to Washington. The firm then spent months trying to divest his stake in the business amid the furore of the Epstein files, ultimately succeeding just weeks before it collapsed.

As well as revelations about Lord Mandelson’s links, the files contained mentions of a meeting between Mr Wegg-Prosser and Epstein in 2010, two years after the latter’s conviction for soliciting prostitution and soliciting a minor.

Epstein died in New York in 2019, while awaiting trial on sex trafficking charges.

Global Counsel’s clients have received extra scrutiny after it emerged that Lord Mandelson failed the enhanced vetting process before his appointment as UK ambassador to Washington, but was given the post anyway after civil servants dismissed the findings. Security concerns are said to have centred around Lord Mandelson’s links to China.

WuXi AppTec, a Chinese pharmaceutical company targeted in a US national security crackdown, was Global Counsel’s biggest client for two years running. The business generated revenue of £1.42m for Global Counsel in 2024. It topped that last year with revenue of £2.24m. Lord Mandelson did not work on the account. WuXi AppTec has denied the US allegations against it.

data.title: Self-checkouts fuelling shoplifting crisis, says M&S boss
data.description:
The chairman of Marks & Spencer claims self-checkouts are fuelling a rise in shoplifting among “good, honest people”.

Archie Norman told The Telegraph that a boom in self-service checkouts at supermarkets had broken the “human link” between shoppers and retailers.

He said: “When normally good, honest people come in and they’re buying their shopping and it doesn’t scan, and there’s nobody manning the checkouts, they’re saying: ‘It’s not my fault and I don’t have much time so if I can’t get my strawberries through, I’ll just put them in my basket’.”

M&S has added hundreds of self-service machines across its stores. It said in 2023 that it had installed 800 of the tills in just 12 months to help it to reach its £150m cost-saving target.

The supermarket began adding self-checkouts into changing rooms in 2024. Last year, it started trialling hi-tech self-checkouts for clothes that identify what is in someone’s basket without the need to scan barcodes.

Mr Norman said retailers did not “have to bring back in-person checkouts” to clamp down on theft. However, he said: “It does mean you’ve got to make the technology easier for people to use.”

His comments follow warnings that crime rates are picking up again across British high streets.

M&S said earlier this month that attacks were becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive” after mobs of youths were captured on video raiding one of its stores in Clapham, London.

Thinus Keeve, the M&S retail director, criticised Sir Sadiq Khan, the London Mayor, for failing to get a grip on crime in the capital, claiming the situation was “getting worse, not better”.

Mr Norman said on Thursday that self-checkouts were not to blame for the riots in Clapham. He blamed uncooperative police forces for failing to intervene against more prolific shoplifters, who he said were “clearing shelves to feed a habit”.

Mr Norman said: “When you have gangs of kids coming in and sweeping the shelves, that’s a police event and it requires an active police response.

“When something like that starts to become common it says to everybody, including ordinary citizens, that it’s not safe.”

Retail chiefs have been urging police to take a more active role in tackling store crime. Simon Roberts, the chief executive of Sainsbury’s, said on Thursday that it would be “a really good thing to see” more police walking around its stores.

Mr Roberts said: “It would make the point that this issue is really serious, it really matters and it’s really top of the agenda.”

Official data this week showed more than half a million shoplifting incidents were reported to police last year. The 509,566 offences represented a 1pc fall from 2024’s level, according to Office for National Statistics figures.

However, retail bosses argue that these figures do not reflect the full picture, given they only reflect those cases that are flagged to the police. Low prosecution rates have meant some retailers have stopped reporting shoplifting incidents.

Lucy Whing, at the British Retail Consortium, said the industry group’s own data indicated roughly 10 times as many thefts were committed against stores, with 5.5 million incidents last year.

She said the rise in organised crime was “particularly worrying as gangs systematically target one store after another across the country”. She called on the police to “make full use” of new powers to crack down on retail crime.

Rising cost of shoplifting

Lord Richard Walker, the Iceland chairman, on Thursday claimed the supermarket was losing “£20m a year on shoplifting”.

Lord Walker told LBC that the rise in shoplifting was fuelling the cost of living crisis by eating into workers’ wages and “investment in lowering prices”.

He also called on the Information Commissioner’s Office to “be on our side” by making greater use of facial recognition technology.

Some retailers have responded to a rise in shoplifting by installing so-called “receipt gates” which require shoppers to scan their receipts to exit.

However, Mr Norman ruled out such a move at the Retail Technology Show in London on Thursday, telling audiences that installing gates would contradict the retailer’s philosophy.

He said: “That’s not us. M&S is a trusted environment – and a safe place to shop.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: If Scott Mills was the dreary uncle, Sara Cox is like your fun, fag-smoking auntie
data.description:
Radio 2’s relationship with its Breakfast Show hosts in the past few years has been a bit like a Hollywood romcom. The unlucky-in-love radio station has bounced between the warm and mumsy (Zoe Ball) and the dogged but dreary (Scott Mills), only to find that the perfect match was there all along – the faithful, funny best pal who’s always on hand with a glass of wine. So Radio 2 stalwart Sara Cox, after Mills’s precipitous fall from grace, has been given the keys to the nation’s most prestigious radio show. It’s a sensible move from the BBC, which must be wondering why it overlooked Cox for Mills in the first place.

That’s not to say that Cox is a “safe pair of hands” (we said that, derisorily, about Mills when he was appointed). She began her career on Channel 4’s controversial late-night “ladette” programme The Girlie Show, a lewd and loud cavalcade of smut, booze and lads with their tops off. As a presenter on The Big Breakfast and then the Radio 1 Breakfast Show, the earthy Boltonian was a tabloid poster girl for lairy late nights. However, Cox has emerged from the Wild West of the Nineties/Noughties media world as one of the good guys.

Her journey has mirrored that of many Radio 2 listeners (indeed, Cox could be called the apex “Mood Mum”). Since the wild-child years, she’s married, had children, and become an author, as well as hosting BBC Two’s literary show Between the Covers. Last month she raised £11.5m for Children in Need by running 135 miles in five days. However, what gives Cox her edge is that she has never pretended to be squeaky clean. This is, after all, the woman who, on Radio 1, gave us details of how she lost her virginity in a field of sheep. The skeletons in her closet were all out partying till the small hours in the late 1990s. Cox is, in other words, normal.

Having been a stand-in on the Breakfast Show many times, as well as spending years as an exceedingly popular host of Radio 2’s Drive Time slot, we know what we’ll get with Cox in the mornings. Unlike Mills, she won’t overthink it and she won’t be anything other than herself – lively, a little fruity, a bit messy, extremely likeable. Of all the probable candidates for the role, Cox was the one who you would most like to go for a pint with – like your favourite fag-smoking auntie. She is, in many ways, BBC Radio’s Claudia Winkleman – a fizzy, impish presence who always played second fiddle to the more polished presenters but who bided their time and got their reward. From the summer, mornings on Radio 2 will be that bit brighter.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Transport watchdog director investigated over train ticket expenses
data.description:
Britain’s taxpayer-funded transport watchdog is investigating a member of its board over allegations he wrongly obtained student railcard discounts and double-claimed travel expenses.

Transport Focus, which represents passengers and is funded mainly by a grant from the Department for Transport, is reviewing payments to Rob Wilson, a non-executive director at the watchdog and a former Tory minister. The Cabinet Office is also investigating his expenses.

Records released under the Freedom of Information Act and seen by The Telegraph show that Mr Wilson claimed the cost of two journeys from both Transport Focus and one of his other publicly funded employers.

In November last year, the records show Mr Wilson claimed a £71.30 train ticket and £26 of parking from both Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission, a quango sponsored by the Cabinet Office, where he is deputy chairman.

He told each organisation he was travelling for a separate event related to his work for them. Mr Wilson reported to Transport Focus that the expenses were related to a board meeting and to the Social Mobility Commission that they were to attend a “higher education summit”.

The records show he submitted the same picture of the receipt for the Reading to London return ticket for both claims.

Mr Wilson also claimed twice for a £68.20 Reading to London return ticket and £26 parking charge in September 2024. On this occasion, he charged the expenses to Transport Focus and the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), an organisation funded from water bills, where he is chairman.

Again, Mr Wilson gave each organisation different reasons for the expenses. He told Transport Focus that he had travelled for a board meeting and the CCW that he had a meeting with the chairman of Anglian Water.

The Telegraph last month reported on Mr Wilson’s expenses records at the CCW, which showed he had obtained discounts on two first-class train tickets worth a third of their cost using a 16-25 railcard.

The discount brought the cost of first-class tickets under the cost of standard class, making them allowable under the CCW’s expenses policy. The organisation said the former MP, who is 61, had selected the option by mistake in two instances.

His expenses records at Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission indicate he made similar errors in his other roles.

He billed Transport Focus for £38.29 last April after booking two journeys with a 16-25 railcard discount. The same month, he also charged the Cabinet Office, which sponsors the Social Mobility Commission, £101.49 for three discounted journeys.

Transport Focus, which last year paid Mr Wilson a fee of £14,000, confirmed this weekend that his expenses claims are under investigation. Claiming a railcard discount fraudulently is a criminal offence and attracts a fine of up to £1,000.

A spokesman said: “We have a process ongoing which reviewed information we received in late March. The process is continuing, reflecting information that has emerged more recently. It would not be appropriate to comment further until that work is complete.”

A Cabinet Office spokesman said: “We were unaware of a duplicate claim by Mr Wilson with Transport Focus until the issue was raised by The Telegraph. We will be investigating this further.”

Lawyers for Mr Wilson said the railcard discounts again “arose from a mistake in his use of the relevant booking app” and its repetition was the result of a default setting.

They added that his double claiming was accidental and that he had intended to split the cost between his employers. Mr Wilson’s lawyers said he was investigating the matter and would ensure that any overpayment was reimbursed.

Mr Wilson, who left Parliament in 2017, also had difficulty selecting the right options while booking flights, according to his Transport Focus records. In 2020 and 2023, he booked and expensed British Airways flights as the Honourable Robert Wilson and The Right Honourable Robert Wilson.

The former honorific is typically used by sitting MPs to refer to each other in parliamentary debates but otherwise mostly applied to the children of viscounts. The Right Honourable is reserved for senior peers and sitting MPs who are members of the Privy Council, on which Mr Wilson never served.

Mr Wilson’s lawyers said the title was mistakenly added to his flight bookings by an administrator. He “had since engaged with British Airways to ensure that this error is corrected”.

It is not the first time that Mr Wilson has encountered controversy over his expenses. In the MPs’ expenses scandal, he was revealed to have claimed nine pence from taxpayers for a 352-yard car journey. He defended the claim, saying the cost of travel “does add up”.

data.title: Zuckerberg cuts thousands of jobs to ‘offset’ AI spending spree
data.description:
Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta is cutting thousands of jobs to “offset” the cost of its multibillion-dollar spending spree on artificial intelligence.

Meta, which owns Facebook, WhatsApp and Instagram, announced plans on Thursday to cut 10pc of staff to help fund heavy investments into AI technologies.

The redundancies will see Mr Zuckerberg’s tech company axe around 8,000 employees next month. Meta is also scrapping plans to hire around 6,000 new staff.

In a memo sent to Meta staff and seen by Bloomberg, executives said: “We’re doing this as part of our continued effort to run the company more efficiently and to allow us to offset the other investments we’re making.”

Meta has warned investors that it could spend up to $169bn (£125bn) this year as it strives to catch up with rivals such as OpenAI and Google in the cut-throat Silicon Valley AI arms race.

It is spending heavily on data centres and AI infrastructure, while Mr Zuckerberg has also offered $100m signing bonuses and multi-year deals worth a reported $1bn in a bid to lure AI talent to Meta.

The plans follow a series of smaller rounds of lay-offs at Meta in recent months, including at its Reality Labs division where 10pc of staff were cut, equivalent to around 700 workers. That reflected Mr Zuckerberg’s unsuccessful bet on the metaverse as the future of the company several years ago.

Analysts said Meta could cut even more jobs later this year as it uses AI tools to automate much of the work currently carried out by employees.

Dan Ives, of Wedbush, said Meta was likely to use “AI tools to automate tasks that once required large teams, allowing the company to streamline operations and reduce costs while maintaining productivity”.

Meta’s plans come amid widespread lay-offs throughout the tech industry as executives funnel money towards AI tools they hope can help people work more efficiently and reduce the need for large workforces.

On Thursday, Microsoft said it was offering voluntary redundancy to thousands of its US employees for the first time in its history. The software giant plans to make the offers in early May to about 8,750 people, or 7pc of its US workforce.

Amy Coleman, Microsoft’s chief people officer, announced the voluntary retirement plan in a memo to staff seen by CNBC.

“Our hope is that this program gives those eligible the choice to take that next step on their own terms, with generous company support,” it said.

While it made no mention of AI, the unusual early retirement scheme comes as Microsoft also ploughs billions of dollars into the technology. The company is a key partner and investor in ChatGPT-developer OpenAI.

Microsoft already cut 15,000 staff last year and committed to spending $140bn this year as part of an expensive bet on AI.

The early retirement offers will be available to employees whose age and years of service at Microsoft total at least 70.

Shares in Meta and Microsoft were largely unchanged in after-hours trade on Wall Street.

Meta declined to comment. Microsoft was contacted for comment.

Economists have warned that AI could reshape global economies, leading to widespread job losses across the world as the technology rapidly automates office-based tasks.

Ironically, the tech sector has so far been one of the hardest hit as chief executives bet that AI can automate large amounts of coding and other tasks. Tech companies have announced more than 90,000 job cuts so far this year, according to industry tracker Layoffs.fyi.

The figure is on track to easily eclipse last year’s total of just over 124,000 redundancies.

In one of the most aggressive rounds of cuts, Jack Dorsey, the founder of Twitter, announced plans last month to axe almost 50pc of staff at his financial technology business Block.

Mr Dorsey said the scale of the cuts reflected the fact AI “fundamentally changes what it means to build and run a company, and that’s accelerating rapidly”.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Tories pledge to drop blanket 20mph speed limits
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said on Thursday that Left-wing councils have turned 20mph into the “national speed of bureaucracy”.

Vowing they would be eliminated by Conservative-led councils, he said: “We are ending blanket limits that slow whole towns to a crawl while doing little for real safety.

“Town halls have spent years waging a war on the very people who get up early, drive to work and keep the economy turning.

“Only the Conservatives are prepared to end the war on drivers, rip up the blanket bans, and get Britain moving again for a stronger economy.”

Existing 20mph measures will be reviewed, Mr Holden said, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The announcement comes before the council elections on May 7.

Labour-run Wales is the main example of a blanket 20mph speed limit being imposed on Britain’s drivers.

The devolved government in Cardiff introduced and then partially abandoned a blanket 20mph policy on all local roads. It would become the single most unpopular law in the Senedd’s brief history.

It also cost the policy’s architect, Welsh Labour’s Lee Waters, his political career after he resigned in the wake of the backlash to its introduction.

Although Ken Skates, his successor, announced that the blanket ban would be lifted, few councils have raised their speed limits to 30mph. Only in areas outside schools and hospitals must the speed limit remain at 20mph in Wales.

The experiment left half a million people facing fines for breaking the lowered limit, as The Telegraph reported last year.

Current government guidance to councils, last updated in March 2024 – before Labour’s general election victory in July that year – says: “Traffic authorities should use the right speed limits in the right places. They should keep their speed limits under review and only introduce 20mph limits and zones in the right places, over time and with local support in urban areas and built-up village streets that are primarily residential.”

The Conservatives’ Plan for Drivers, published in 2023, set out the principle that 20mph limits should be targeted and evidence-led rather than imposed as “blanket measures”, a party spokesman said.

Mr Holden added: “The previous Conservative government was clear in our guidance that blanket approaches were wrong. Labour ignored it and their councils kept rolling them out in the teeth of public opposition.”

data.title: Losses hit £53m at The Sun as it battles falling tabloid sales
data.description:
The Sun has reported a £53m loss as Rupert Murdoch’s newspaper battles falling tabloid sales.

Newly filed accounts for News Group Newspapers (NGN), which publishes The Sun and Sun on Sunday, show pre-tax losses widened from £18m in the year to July 2025.

It came as turnover fell by 7pc to £273m, which bosses blamed on a drop in print sales and algorithm changes that dented online readership.

The figures underscore the challenge facing The Sun, which has been grappling with how to restore profitability across its advertising-funded business model.

The Sun introduced a premium model last year, offering readers access to exclusive stories and columns by star writers such as Jeremy Clarkson for £2 a month.

The move, which mirrored a similar partial paywall launched by MailOnline, was aimed at reducing the title’s reliance on dwindling ad revenues.

However, the rollout of AI tools in Google’s search engine is posing a fresh threat to free-to-access online journalism, with a number of publishers already reporting sharp reductions in traffic.

The tabloid has also sought to offset declining readership by expanding in the US, while it has also made redundancies to cut costs.

The publisher employed 530 jobs at the end of the last financial year, down from 552 a year previously.

The Sun reported increased legal costs related to the phone-hacking scandal at The News of the World, which more than doubled last year to £36.7m.

This is thought to include a £10m settlement with the Duke of Sussex, which was struck with NGN last year. The publisher issued an apology for “serious intrusion” by The Sun and phone hacking by private investigators working for the News of the World.

The troubles at The Sun stand in contrast to Mr Murdoch’s broadsheet titles, which operate under a subscription model.

Revenues at The Times and Sunday Times increased to £390m, driven by growth in subscribers and advertising. The newspaper has been seeking to boost reader numbers by reducing the total number of stories it publishes, in a strategy it claims should result in “better stories”.

Decline of TalkTV

Separate accounts showed Mr Murdoch had lost more than £150m on his failed news channel TalkTV in a rare setback for the media mogul.

TalkTV, the opinionated broadcaster that was pulled off air two years ago, posted an operating loss of £8.7m in the year to July 2025.

It takes cumulative losses since the channel’s launch in 2022 to more than £150m. Mr Murdoch’s News Corp also booked a £25m writedown on its investments in the UK, which it said was in response to challenging market conditions affecting its broadcasting operations.

A £154m loan to TalkTV has been waived and the channel’s parent company has now ceased trading.

The demise of TalkTV marks a rare defeat for the nonagenarian tycoon, who last year handed control of his news empire to his eldest son, Lachlan Murdoch, after a bitter succession battle.

The deal will secure the Right-wing stance of Mr Murdoch’s outlets, which include Fox and the Wall Street Journal as well as his UK newspapers. Lachlan is closely aligned with his father’s politics, whereas siblings James, Elisabeth and Prudence are viewed as more liberal.

Mr Murdoch was separately handed a boost this week as a US judge dismissed Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.4bn) lawsuit against The Wall Street Journal for publishing a lewd birthday note sent by the president to Jeffrey Epstein.

A spokesman for News UK said: “News UK is a portfolio of assets, each at a different level of digital maturity. 

“Those brands are managed as a whole and our investments are focused on formats which will deliver revenues over the long term to create a sustainable future for all of our journalism.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Billionaire Weston family to spin off Primark
data.description:
The billionaire family behind Primark is to spin off the fashion chain from its food business, breaking up one of Britain’s biggest consumer conglomerates after 65 years.

Associated British Foods (ABF) said it planned to split Primark off by the end of 2027, paving the way for a separate listing on the FTSE 100.

ABF is listed on the London Stock Exchange but around 60pc of its shares are owned by the Weston family’s Wittington Investments. The conglomerate is one of the world’s biggest sugar producers and also owns brands including Ovaltine, Hovis and Twinings.

Analysts have predicted that a separate listing of Primark, which has 486 stores across 19 markets, could see it valued at a similar level to rivals such as Next, which is worth more than £16bn.

George Weston, the chief executive and a member of the controlling Weston family, will become chief executive of the food business, while Eoin Tonge will continue to lead Primark who took over as interim chief executive last year.

Mr Weston said there were “a lot of good things that will come from this separation”, adding that Primark was “very capable of standing on its own two feet” and would benefit from having a board of non-executives who had specific retail experience.

He said ABF’s food business, meanwhile, had been “under appreciated because most of the interest has inevitably been around Primark”.

Plans to press ahead with the separation come despite warnings from ABF that the war in Iran was likely to hit consumer spending.

Mr Weston said there was a “risk to Primark sales if the conflict persists and consumer spending deteriorates”.

“Based on the last couple of weeks, we’ve seen sales right across Europe reduce, but we just don’t know,” he said. “Some of our customers may stop shopping, but some other customers might start shopping at Primark because they see value there.”

Mr Weston said inflation from the Middle East conflict was likely to be “manageable” this year. ABF had locked in energy prices in advance, helping to limit the impact of the shock.

By July and August, though, he said ABF would “become increasingly exposed to the higher costs, should they still be in place”.

The situation “might resolve itself before those extra costs come through the system”, he added.

Mr Weston said ABF had not yet seen any disruption to shipments. One vessel carrying its goods passed through the Suez Canal last weekend. Some freight companies have been avoiding the Egyptian waterway over fears of spillover from the conflict nearby.

Shares in ABF slumped as much as 5pc to £17.90 on Tuesday. Analysts at Barclays said this was likely driven by the “cautious commentary” about Primark sales, as well as weaker-than-expected profits in its sugar business.

ABF posted a 2pc fall in revenue to just under £9.5bn in the 24 weeks ending February, while profit declined by 9pc to £632m.

data.title: Leonard Licht, brilliant stock-picker for pension funds at Warburgs and Mercury Asset Management
data.description:
Leonard Licht, who has died aged 81, was one of London’s most influential but idiosyncratic investors as a senior executive of Mercury Asset Management.

Mercury began life as the investment division of the merchant bank SG Warburg & Co, which Leonard Licht joined as a teenage clerk in 1963. He went on to make a name as a brilliant stock-picker and to be co-leader of the firm’s pension fund team – the most powerful in the City – when Mercury was floated as a separate company in 1987.

Licht’s major clients, for whom he produced outstanding returns over the years, were the pension funds of the BBC and British Rail; the latter, according to a colleague, “thought he walked on water”. Combining encyclopaedic knowledge of British companies with an intuitive sense of value and an intolerance for stockbrokers’ waffle, he bought shares he liked in unusually large portions.

These included significant stakes in the shipping and property group P&O, Burton Menswear, House of Fraser department stores and the chinaware manufacturer Wedgwood, of which Mercury at one time held almost a quarter. When a clutch of privatised regional water companies floated simultaneously on the stock exchange in December 1989, Licht bought 10 per cent of the whole list.

Exercising emotional intelligence rather than dry analysis, Licht won new clients not with slideshows on investment strategy but by telling them simply that he preferred shares “which go up”. His picks did so far more often than they let him down.                

Leonard Samuel Licht was born in London on March 15 1945, the only child of Bernhard Licht and his wife Hilde, née Muller. Bernhard’s family had been furniture manufacturers at Glogau in Lower Silesia (then in Germany, now in Poland), but the couple fled the Nazi regime in 1938 to arrive in England with 10 shillings each. Bernhard was interned on the Isle of Man in 1940 but went on to build a business with his brother Herbert as export agents for textile manufacturers.

Leonard was educated at Christ’s College in Finchley, where by his own account he was a “very idle” pupil. He nevertheless became an avid reader and, “just after I learned to walk”, a collector first of bus tickets of different colours according to the fare and later, encouraged by birthday presents from a grandfather, of stamps.

Leaving school in 1963 with one A-level, Licht wrote on spec to ask for a job at Warburgs – where he had a family introduction – and was taken on as a clerk at £360 a year. His first task for the investment team was to file company reports and Extel cards, the principal source of financial updates in the pre-electronic age, and to man the phones during the short lunchtime permitted under austere Warburg rules.

Though he enjoyed spotting curiosities in the daily data flow, Licht was an awkward employee who twice survived being asked to leave the firm before he found a niche as an assistant fund manager and went on to join the team responsible for Warburgs’ growing pension business.

He operated in a style all his own, commuting to work in a chauffeured Rolls-Royce. Prone to temper and mood swings, his impatience with a Hong Kong tailor’s tape measure caused a consignment of 10 bespoke suits to be dismissed as “a disaster” and passed to his chauffeur, whom they did not fit at all.

If Licht loved and to some extent personified the culture of Warburgs and Mercury, he was neither a company man nor a conventional team player. He particularly disliked committee meetings and the increasingly burdensome bureaucracy of compliance. Though he eventually held the title of vice-chairman, he was best left flying solo.

In the summer of 1992 he took his co-directors by surprise when he abruptly resigned to join Jupiter Tyndall, an up-and-coming fund boutique – led by John Duffield, whose maverick investing style matched his own – in which Licht had bought a small stake on Mercury’s behalf a few months earlier.

Mercury declined to present Licht’s departure as amicable, though personal friendships were restored after a period of coolness. Nor were his three years with Jupiter notably happy; he resigned in 1996 to pursue his own interests, later returning to the fray as chairman of HG, a private equity business that had originated in Mercury before being spun out by Merrill Lynch, which bought Mercury in 1997.

Younger HG colleagues warmly recalled his influenc​e and encouragement, and the enjoyment of outings with him to Borough Market.

Among Licht’s private investments was a stake in Falkland Island Holdings, whose assets included the port of Stanley and its only hotel. After retiring, he told friends he had sold all his shares and returned to his passion for stamps and coins.

He and his wife Judith, née Grossman, whom he married in 1973, were a devoted couple. They lived for some years in Monaco before returning to London, and wintered in Miami Beach. She survives him with their son and daughter.        

Leonard Licht, born March 15 1945, died March 25 2026​

data.title: Thames Water to escape new penalties until 2030 under rescue deal
data.description:
Thames Water is set to escape new fines until 2030 under a deal agreed between creditors and the industry watchdog to save the stricken company.

Ofwat, which regulates the sector, is planning to shield the struggling utility from financial penalties linked to poor performance in return for a commitment from creditors to invest cash in the business for the next four years.

The controversial plan would carve out Thames from a penalty scheme run by Ofwat which levies fines if water companies fall behind on key metrics such as leaks and sewer flooding.

Thames is struggling under a £20bn debt pile and will run out of money again in October without the new creditor agreement.

However, the penalties deal has angered campaigners, who say the watchdog is failing in its duty to hold water companies to account.

Feargal Sharkey, the clean water campaigner, accused Ofwat of shirking its responsibility to regulate the industry.

“Ofwat has proved yet again it is incapable, unwilling to and simply does not want to properly regulate the greed, the abuse of customers and the financial engineering that is destroying this industry and the environment,” he said.

The plan, which has been put forward by creditors including US hedge funds Elliott Management and Silver Point, first went to Ofwat for approval in June 2025.

The creditors, known as London & Valley Water, provided the utility with £3bn in emergency funding last year.

As part of the deal, some creditors like Elliott would accept a 30pc write-down in their debt while more junior creditors would lose all their money.

The creditors defended the agreement and said it would mean “clear accountability” if things went wrong.

A spokesman said: “All outstanding fines will be paid. Regulators will have enhanced transparency and Thames Water will have clear accountability for reducing pollution and improving environmental outcomes against stretching performance targets.”

Utilities such as Thames Water have to pay penalties when they fail to meet performance targets set by Ofwat for the year.

Under the new proposals, first reported by The Financial Times, these penalties would be replaced with a formal commitment to invest in Thames Water.

This would allow Ofwat to continue with investigations and penalties but any cash raised from them would be spent on fixing issues with the business rather than returned to the Treasury.

The utility could still face separate fines from other regulators like the Environment Agency and would still have to reckon with possible legal cases. All outstanding fines would also have to be paid.

The Government is reportedly keen to keep Thames Water out of temporary nationalisation but the utility has only been kept afloat so far by the £3bn emergency loan, which is expected to cost the utility an extra £800m in interest payments.

Thames Water said that if Ofwat agreed a deal, creditors had offered the company an additional £6.6bn of debt at a lower rate of interest than the emergency loan, as well as £3.35bn of equity.

A spokesman for Thames Water said: “Thames Water remains focused on securing a market-led solution which delivers improvements for customers and the environment as soon as practicable, whilst making progress with our operational and financial turnaround plan.”

An Ofwat spokesperson said: “We continue to engage with London & Valley Water, and are reviewing their plans carefully to assess whether they deliver a turnaround in the company’s operational performance and strengthen its financial resilience to the benefit of customers and the environment.”

data.title: Act now on energy or grocery bills will rise, Sainsbury’s warns Reeves
data.description:
The cost of a weekly shop will rise unless Rachel Reeves helps food manufacturers with spiralling energy prices, the boss of Sainsbury’s has warned.

Simon Roberts, the chief executive of Britain’s second largest supermarket, urged the Chancellor to intervene with further support despite a separate warning from Marks & Spencer that it was “too late” to prevent prices rising.

Mr Roberts said: “The single biggest thing the Government could do to help support keeping food prices down is to make sure that energy costs in the industry are not rising faster.

“Now is the time for the Government to really look at what it can do in the food sector to keep those pressures contained as far as possible.”

Food inflation hit 3.7pc in March, but the Food and Drink Federation has warned that it could reach 10pc by the end of this year, fuelled by the Iran war.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran has driven up the price of oil and gas, which will filter through into everything from greenhouse costs for vegetable growers to transportation of food and drink to supermarkets.

Archie Norman, the chairman of M&S, said: “You can’t see any trace of the Strait of Hormuz in shops today, but obviously cost increases are going to seep through the system.

“We all rely on sea freight, air freight and diesel. That’s our cost to carry.”

Speaking at a conference, he said: “I expect by September to see price inflation ticking up. Even if it stops today, it’s too late.”

Mr Roberts declined to speculate on how high inflation would go, but said: “There’s no doubt there’ll be pressure on food prices, given that energy is such a key component of what goes into the food we eat every day.”

He urged the Chancellor to broaden existing support packages for energy-intensive businesses to include food makers and retailers.

A government package unveiled last week will support more than 10,000 manufacturers in sectors such as steel and concrete. It will reduce their electricity bills by up to 25pc by axing green levies, though the support only takes effect next year.

Mr Roberts said widening support to cover the food industry would go “some way” to keeping a lid on rising grocery prices.

He said: “We ask for that to include food and food manufacturing parts of the industry ... We’ve made the point that anything we can do to bring down the cost of energy in the food system is going to help mitigate the impact of inflation.”

The comments come weeks after supermarket bosses met with the Chancellor to warn over the risk of rising food prices.

At the time, bosses told Ms Reeves that she needed to look at “domestic policy levels” to cool inflation. However, the Chancellor has yet to lay out support for the industry.

The Sainsbury’s chief said there were “a number of areas we discussed and I think it’s very clear that all of those need to be looked at and considered”.

He was speaking as the supermarket said its profits could drop to as low as £975m this year, down from £1.025bn last year, amid uncertainty over the Middle East conflict.

Mr Roberts said: “The conflict in the Middle East will impact both our customers and our business. The duration and extent of these impacts is very uncertain and this is reflected in our profit guidance.”

Shares in Sainsbury’s fell almost 5pc on the London Stock Exchange.

Separately, WH Smith warned over the impact of the war in Iran, slashing its profit forecasts for the year to the end of August to between £90m and £105m. It previously forecast profits to come in between £100m and £115m.

The retailer, which runs stores in airports, said it was now “taking a more cautious outlook reflecting the impact on passenger numbers and weaker consumer confidence”.

Shares in WH Smith plunged by 9pc.

It follows a warning from airlines this week over the risk of travel chaos this summer as they grapple with dwindling supplies of aviation fuel. The Government has been told to prepare for fuel shortages now if disruption to flights is to be avoided.

data.title: Britain’s £8bn bet on the developing world
data.description:
Britain will invest up to £8bn in companies across the developing world over the next five years, in a bid to make a return for the UK taxpayer while driving global development.

The initiative is being led by British International Investment (BII), a government owned UK investment vehicle founded in 1948 to boost agricultural production across the empire.

At the time, Britain faced food shortages and rationing in the wake of the Second World War.

Jenny Chapman, the UK Minister for Development, now wants to accelerate the flow of capital to developing countries via BII as UK overseas direct aid budgets are shrinking.

It is envisaged that for every pound of public money BII invests a matched contribution will come from the private sector, bringing the total investment to about £15 billion.

Leslie Maasdorp, the South Africa born Chief Executive of BII, said: “Britain has a proud history of acting as a global leader in international development. BII is committed to demonstrating that a new, mutually-beneficial path exists that creates secure jobs and stable economic conditions in the countries in which we invest, while providing value for money for the UK taxpayer”.

Investing in developing economies is high risk but can also bring in substantial financial rewards beyond the help it provides locally in terms of innovation and jobs.

Over the last decade, BII generated a £1.6 billion return on investments, equating to a return of around five per cent a year. The new five year cycle envisages a minimum return on capital of at least two per cent but managers will be hoping for more.

The fund’s capital will also support UK based companies that are delivering positive economic and environmental outcomes in developing markets.

In an interview with the Telegraph, Mr Maasdorp said that development and national security were “reinforcing” concepts and “not opposites”. 

“The sources of uncontrolled migration flows, the sources of illicit finance, the sources of terrorism, extremism… they come from these challenging environments,” he said.

“If the global north is not engaged in investing in and stabilising these places, it impacts on the well being of the UK because the world is so interconnected.

“We cannot solve the global challenges we all face – poverty, instability, conflict and global public health – without bringing the least developed countries with us on the journey to shared prosperity.”

China has become the world’s biggest state investor in developing economies over the last 20 years, making huge investments in infrastructure across Asia and Africa.

Its strategy has been guided by the Belt and Road initiative, which aims to forge new trade corridors and dramatically boost China’s influence in global affairs.

All over Africa, Chinese construction companies with excess capacity and expertise developed during China’s own boom have been building ports, dams, roads, railways, schools, hospitals and solar power systems across the continent.

Mr Maasdorp said the approach to investment being pursued by BII and other western state-backed investors was different to the Chinese approach. 

It was not “state-led” and, while UK companies could be utilised where appropriate, BII’s strategy was not dependent on them. 

“Our model is embedded in the belief that the private sector is the engine of economic growth,” he said. “The state has an important and vital role to play in creating enabling conditions for investment… but ultimately it is the private sector and entrepreneurs that make an economy tick”. 

He added that long term “strategic relationships” with similar state-backed investment funds across the west gave BII and its partners the financial heft to compete effectively with China, he added.

BII has the freedom to invest in any enterprise that drives development and provides a public good, but it focuses on those countries most in need of investment.

Between 2026-2031, at least 40 per cent of BII’s total new commitments by value will be in climate finance – a sector that China has become dangerously dominant in globally.

BII is also a founding member of the 2X Challenge which has raised over $33.6 billion to empower women’s economic development.

But its assets are varied.

Current investments include the financing of a new deepwater container port in the Congo, an extension of the partnership that commenced with the modernisation and expansion of ports in Senegal, Egypt and Somaliland.

The new port development is expected to enable the creation of approximately 85,000 jobs and around $1.12 billion in additional trade and $429m in increased economic output annually.

BII was also a founder investor in Safaricom Ethiopia which was awarded Ethiopia’s first private mobile network licence.

Since its launch in 2022, the cost of mobile data services in the country has fallen by up to 70 per cent and, by June 2024, the company had signed up 4.4 million customers. 

Like an increasing number of international investors, Mr Maasdorp is bullish on the long term prospects for the continent of Africa – the world’s last great underdeveloped region.

It has the world’s fastest growing population, huge mineral wealth and enough farmland to become the “breadbasket of the world,” he says.

Its governance has improved dramatically over the last 25 years and, if African economies can “add value” by processing what they produce rather than simply exporting, it will boom just as the so-called Asian Tigers did in the 1990s.

“There are lots of pockets of positive momentum which could change the prospects of Africa all around,” said Mr Maasdorp.

 “With an average age of 18 or 19, the energy of youth is marked. And what the world of social media has done is give people a sense of what is possible – aspiration.

“Tech and AI companies are developing now in Africa, which I think holds great potential for many sectors to leapfrog ahead as they are building, in many cases, from scratch.”

Protect yourself and your family by learning more about Global Health Security
