data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: Why Britain faces the growing risk of Spanish-style blackouts
data.description:
The great British blackout was once a threat that hung over the country during the depths of winter, when generators struggled to meet demand or storms destroyed power lines.

But today, as the net zero revolution rolls on, the greatest threat to grid stability could land on a sunny Sunday afternoon.

According to the National Energy System Operator (Neso), the body responsible for keeping the lights on, the UK power grid is at increasing risk of being overwhelmed by surges in solar power.

Underpinning this threat is the fact that too many solar panels have been connected to the grid without any way of monitoring them or controlling their output.

As a result, a typically sunny day in summer can lead to nearly 19 gigawatts (GW) of power pouring into the UK’s networks, while only 12-15GW is being consumed.

That imbalance is a recipe for disaster because significant destabilisation of the grid can cause blackouts.

Such a calamity struck Spain and Portugal last year, when a chain reaction triggered by solar farms knocked out power to 55 million people.

At the time, solar and wind generated 80pc of energy in the Iberian Peninsula, the result of renewables displacing power stations run by nuclear and gas.

While a welcome switch for supporters of green energy, the removal of traditional power stations can prove problematic – as Spain and Portugal discovered last year.

This is because of their importance in providing vital system stability, known as inertia.

Inertia is created by generators with spinning parts – such as turbines running on gas, coal or hydropower – which wind and solar do not have.

This form of kinetic energy helps to prevent sudden changes in a system’s frequency.

Therefore, when two solar farms suddenly disconnected from Spain’s grid last year, there was no steady inertia to keep the country’s power networks afloat.

In practice, Neso is confident the UK will never see such a disaster. However, it admits the challenge is growing as ever more solar farms are built in Ed Miliband’s net zero drive.

“We are confident that we have the tools required to operate the system safely and reliably,” Neso said in its Summer Outlook report.

“But there may be periods when balancing supply, demand and system needs will require us to use our full range of operational tools, including system notices.”

However, independent experts are not so sure – and we may soon find out who is right. The crunch will probably come around the late May bank holiday, or on any sunny summer Sunday.

Last year, that date saw national demand for power plummet to a record low of 11GW, while sun and wind drove up power generation to nearly 19GW. And crucially, Neso could do little to stop it.

This sense of powerlessness is new. In the past, the grid operator’s response to an excess of power was simply to switch off some power stations.

But Neso’s problem is that it can no longer see what is happening on the UK’s power networks.

Unlike before, energy is being created naturally from wind and solar farms that Neso cannot control.

Underlying Britain’s energy grid are two main power networks.

At the top is the high-voltage transmission grid, which takes electricity from power stations to cities, towns and industry.

Everything on it is closely measured and monitored from Neso’s Electricity National Control Centre in Wokingham, Berkshire.

There, controllers use a giant wall-to-wall digital map of the transmission network to match supply to demand – switching power stations on or reducing output as needed.

Below that lies the distribution network, which takes electricity from the transmission grid, reduces its voltage, and pumps it into our homes and businesses.

It was designed to be a passive power carrier, meaning it lacks most of the switching or measuring systems found on the transmission grid. Ultimately, this makes it hard to monitor or control.

However, in the rush for decarbonisation, officials have been hooking the distribution system up to hundreds of new power sources, particularly solar farms.

Almost all of those solar generators are largely invisible to Neso’s operators, and they cannot see how much power is being generated or switch it off.

This now poses a problem, according to Simon Gallagher, the chief executive of UK Network Services, a leading consultancy.

“Traditionally, most power generators were connected to the transmission system and ‘dispatched’ centrally, so Neso can instruct them to turn on, turn off, ramp up and down to balance the grid in real time,” he says.

“Big power stations with large gas generators get tuned up and down as Neso monitors changes in demand.

“But solar is almost all connected to the distribution system and Neso has no control or even visibility of what it is doing. It means there is a risk of imbalances. On a sunny summer Sunday, demand will be very low, but solar will be generating at full capacity.”

However, whether this results in a blackout similar to Spain is another matter.

Some industry experts say an energy crisis is unlikely in the UK because its grid is structured differently, but regional failures and high costs remain a threat.

“Britain’s solar surplus problem is more about balancing costs than sudden system failure,” says Timothy Santonastaso, the deputy head of European power pricing at the consultancy Argus.

Such problems should have been addressed sooner, according to Claire Coutinho, the energy secretary in the last Conservative government and now a shadow minister.

‘Bogus numbers’

While in power, Coutinho says she became so worried about the impact of excessive solar disrupting the grid that she ordered civil servants to investigate.

This involved a review of the costs associated with balancing the grid when it is too windy or sunny.

Last year, the Government spent more than £1bn on paying wind farms to switch off in so-called curtailment costs.

“I started a full systems cost of wind and solar in the department because we need to know the true costs of renewables to consumers,” she says.

“I asked officials to prove to me that renewables were cheap and then realised their numbers were bogus.”

Coutinho claims Miliband has since cancelled her review. She also says the Energy Secretary is paying too much for net zero.

A spokesman for Miliband rejected the criticisms, instead claiming that solar and wind farms would bring huge benefits. “We are reversing decades of underinvestment and delivering the biggest upgrade to the grid in decades, which will minimise constraint costs, meet the capacity needed to deliver clean power by 2030 and help bring down bills for good,” he said.

He also played down the threat of grid disruption. “The National Energy System Operator has always had to manage changes in demand between the winter and summer months. They have well-established tools to manage the grid, including during times of low demand, which will not impact supply.”

The fresh row over solar is just the latest front in the battle over green energy.

Within Labour, Miliband’s renewables rollout has delighted the party faithful and made him increasingly popular among members.

However, it has also turned energy and climate policy, an area on which there was general political agreement until two years ago, into a culture war.

Leading the charge against Labour is Reform UK, which has pledged to disconnect solar farms if it wins power at the next general election.

“These solar farms are being built for the benefit of foreign investors, but they are destroying our countryside and ruining our environment,” says Richard Tice, Reform’s energy spokesman.

“It’s my living mission to stop this madness. Britain has done its bit – we’ve cut emissions by 50pc and impoverished ourselves doing it. Now’s the time to make Britain prosperous again, and that means cheap energy, lots of it, whether from gas, nuclear or oil.”

No doubt Miliband will disagree. However, the latest warning from Neso may at least make him sit up and notice.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Trump is an utter disaster for the world’s oil and gas industry
data.description:
The bittersweet joke in energy circles is that the Nobel committee should create a new climate prize to reward Donald Trump for his outstanding services.

Nobody has done more to accelerate the world’s electrotech revolution and pull forward the irreversible collapse of the oil and gas industry – not even Greta Thunberg, Al Gore or the strategic planners of the Chinese Communist Party.

The Carlyle Group made waves last year with a report called the “New Joule Order”. The giant US investment firm argued that seaborne molecules were no longer safe in the Hobbesian new world of America First and maritime anarchy.

Jeff Currie, its energy guru, said the open trading system of the last 80 years had been a function of American and allied power, relying on “the US navy as its muscle”.

This gift to the world, akin to the role of Britain’s Royal Navy in the 19th century, had allowed a constant stream of tankers carrying oil, diesel, naphtha, and liquefied natural gas (LNG) to criss-cross the oceans with scarcely a moment’s thought about choke points, taking for granted the international dollar system that lubricated trade so efficiently.

Currie said the “quest for security” was becoming the dominant driver of electrotech, as countries rushed to insulate themselves from geopolitical risk. It has little to do with climate change and even less to do with the red herring of net zero.

But not even he expected the US to lose its strategic compass so comprehensively as to attack Iran without securing the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off 8pc of total world trade for 48 days, including supplies of fertilisers, helium needed for semiconductors and a host of critical inputs that Washington forgot to think about.

Currie says the elemental contract of the petrodollar system is, or was, that the US would keep the sea lanes open and protect oil and gas producers from all foes. In return, the petrostates would recycle their vast earnings into US Treasuries and Wall Street, letting America enjoy the exorbitant privilege of inexhaustible cheap capital. “The grand bargain has been violated,” he said.

You might think that US oil and gas companies would be celebrating the windfall profits of Trump’s war, but the mood was closer to horror at the recent CeraWeek summit in Houston, the sanctum sanctorum of the drilling industry.

Casey Merriman, from Energy Intelligence, said the mood behind the scenes was “panic and dread” rooted in an awful premonition that the fallout of war would pose “existential risks to the future of oil and gas”.

Data from Ember show that 78pc of humanity lives in countries that are net importers of oil. These states have been bleeding $1.7tn (£1.25tn) a year just to pay oil rents to petrostates or frackers in Texas, and that could double again in short order if oil futures converge upwards to reflect the $140-$150 levels seen in the physical markets.

The importers have put up with this glaring anomaly out of inertia and the well-organised resistance of vested interests. Not any longer.

“Depending on fossil fuels is dangerous,” said Lee Jae-myung, the South Korean president, confessing that matters were now so grave that he could not sleep.

He has called for a fundamental rethink of South Korea’s energy strategy. “This is an emergency situation and we must shift to renewable energy very quickly,” he said.

France is doubling investment in electrification and will ban boilers in new homes at the end of this year. Variants of this debate are going on all over the world.

The double crisis of Trump’s war so soon after Vladimir Putin’s war has shaken the world out of complacency. All can now see that America has become a predator state. They can also see that a drone costing $20,000 is enough to stop a $150m tanker dead in the water.

Kingsmill Bond, Ember’s director of energy, said this shock had hit just as electrotech comes of age and is undercutting legacy fossil fuels on pure cost for power, heat and most land transport across the majority of the world’s populated regions.

“We didn’t have an alternative in past crises. Now we do. We have a suite of completely new solutions that change everything,” he said.

“Solar power coupled with storage is down to $60 [per] MWh across the global sunbelt. It costs $160 [per] MWh right now to cover just the cost of importing variable gas in Asia. It’s game over for LNG in Asia,” he said.

The figures for total net fossil imports as a share of primary energy are revealing, and not what you might think. To pick a few, they are: Japan (84pc), South Korea (80pc), Italy (72pc), Turkey and Spain (70pc), Germany (67pc), France (47pc), India and the UK (37pc) and China (24pc).

Britain is partially shielded by North Sea oil and gas working in tandem with fast-increasing volumes of North Sea wind power. The International Monetary Fund persists in claiming that this country is more vulnerable than peers to a fossil shock. It might usefully revisit its assumptions and stop repeating arrant nonsense.

Bond said the lesson of the two Opec crises in the 1970s is that it took the second shock in 1979 to cause a lasting shift in policy. “Global final oil demand per capita peaked in 1979 and has never recovered,” he said.

The response after the 1970s was to switch to more fuel-efficient cars and break the link between oil and GDP growth, but it could only go so far. This time the wider fossil fuel system faces terminal run-off in the importing countries, swept away by the scramble for safer homemade supply, which now also happens to be cheaper. Obviously, oil and gas will still be needed for jet fuel (for a while) and as a feedstock for petrochemicals.

China is already a post-oil, post-gas, electrostate economy, rolling out more wind, solar and electric vehicles each year than the rest of the world combined – although it has not cracked solid-state batteries for EVs and may yet be outflanked by the West.

What began as a strategic insurance policy against a future US naval blockade of China’s seaborne energy imports has become a juggernaut by now. Sales of EVs and plug-in hybrids were half of the Chinese car market last year. They are running at 60pc this month.

The Chinese near-abroad is now slotting into this new joule order. EV sales are around 40pc in Vietnam and Singapore, 25pc in Thailand, 18pc in Indonesia, and rising.

Laurence Tubiana, the chief convenor of the Paris Climate Accord, said that as recently as five years ago African leaders would bridle at green colonialism, accusing the West of trying to deny the Global South a cheap energy route out of poverty.

Today the mood is radically different. Country after country has done its sums and concluded that it could leapfrog centralised, fossil-based grids and opt instead for solar micro-grids, slashing costs and freeing itself of fuel-import bondage. “They have no interest any longer in being trapped in the old energy system,” she said.

We have not felt the full impact of this crisis yet. Currie said it reminded him of the eerie insouciance in early days of the pandemic. First it hit China and shut the economy: the world reacted as if the virus had nothing to do with them. Then it hit South Korea, then Iran, then Italy, and still stock markets kept rising. It took six weeks before Wall Street suddenly buckled and crashed 35pc along with a seizure in the US Treasury market.

Trump’s war will hit home once the physical barrels stop arriving and cannot be bought for love or money, a moment scheduled for late April once the usable global buffer of 800 million barrels is exhausted.

Currie has another warning as the S&P 500 reached new highs based on nothing more than Truth Social tweets. The US oil and gas companies make up just 3pc of the equity capitalisation of the index; the other 97pc is on the wrong side of the Gulf War trade and will soon suffer the consequences of what remains the biggest energy shock of modern times.

Wars have a bad habit of spinning out of control.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves promises help on soaring factory energy bills – but only in a year’s time
data.description:
British factories face a year-long wait for relief from soaring energy bills despite Rachel Reeves’s suggestion that help is on the way.

The Chancellor promised on Sunday to “set out the next phase of our plans to boost Britain’s competitiveness” in response to high energy costs, which are worsening because of the war in Iran.

But The Telegraph understands that one of the Government’s key measures to cut the country’s crippling industrial energy bills cannot start any earlier than April 2027.

Business groups urged Ms Reeves to move faster, with a wave of inflation already hitting companies. New surveys of confidence and output suggest both have already fallen to lows not seen since the pandemic.

Addressing the “global economic crisis” triggered by war Iran, the Chancellor wrote over the weekend that she would “set out the principles that will guide how we support businesses in the months ahead”.

Ms Reeves is this week expected to outline details of the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which will see green levies on electricity costs waived for about 7,000 companies in eight key sectors.

However, a government source indicated that the Chancellor would not bring forward the timelines for the programme to launch. It means factories face a year-long wait for relief at a time when energy costs are soaring.

‘Essential’ to move faster

The Confederation of British Industry (CBI) lobby group said it was “essential” to move faster. “It needs to be delivered at pace. Any delay risks undermining business confidence at a time when it is already under strain,” said Tania Kumar, the CBI’s director for energy transition.

In a survey of 3,000 British voters conducted by the Institute for Economic Affairs, 77pc wanted the Government to focus on cutting the cost of energy. Similarly large majorities urged the Government to focus on economic growth, and cut taxes on workers and businesses.

In an article in The Sunday Times, the Chancellor said she did not want to be rushed into potentially expensive or ineffective policies.

Her hesitancy came as Donald Trump on Sunday said the US Navy would block the Strait of Hormuz – a vital transit route at least one fifth of the world’s oil, gas and fertiliser – to any ship that had paid a toll to Iran.

This could extend a conflict that has already squeezed energy supply chains and driven up fuel and electricity costs. Brent crude prices jumped as high as $104 a barrel on Monday morning after closing on Friday at $94.

Ian Stewart, the Deloitte chief economist, said the Iran war was “reshaping business sentiment” after a survey of finance chiefs by the consultancy found confidence at a six-year low. The barometer fell to -57pc in the first three months of this year, from minus 13pc the previous quarter.

Mr Stewart said: “It’s created a shock to CFO confidence, lowering optimism to levels we haven’t seen since the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic.

“Finance leaders are coping with high levels of external uncertainty, and their focus is on managing risks from geopolitics, rising energy prices and higher financing costs.”

A separate survey of 4,000 businesses by accountancy firm BDO found output had dropped to a five-year low.

“The decline in business output and confidence underlines the UK’s exposure to the global energy shock,” said Scott Knight, head of growth at BDO.

“Confidence will not be restored until energy flows stabilise under a lasting ceasefire.”

BDO warned the combination of increasing price pressures and falling demand would prompt businesses to stop hiring workers, with employment intentions at a 15-year low.

Britain’s manufacturers were already labouring under the highest electricity prices in Europe, but these will rise still further if the Iran conflict keeps squeezing oil and gas supplies.

Ms Kumar said the Chancellor was “right to resist the lure of short-term, unaffordable sticking plasters”, but she needed to seize this moment to make Britain more cost-competitive.

The BICS is a seven-year scheme to exempt businesses from paying two green levies and an energy-security charge. This could reduce their electricity costs by up to £40 per megawatt hour.

Scheme ‘does not go far enough’

To be eligible, factories must be in economically critical sectors. These include advanced manufacturing, clean energy, defence, digital technologies and life sciences. Suppliers to these sectors, such as makers of chemicals, steel or critical minerals, can also qualify.

Responding to a consultation on the BICS that ended in January, the Aluminium Federation said its members had “consistently reported that the proposed April 2027 implementation date is far too late”.

The Chemicals Industry Association told the Government that the scheme was not as generous as others in the EU, and “does not go far enough to make the UK a globally competitive manufacturing location”.

Other business groups supported the BICS but warned the government not to fund the scheme by increasing levies on companies that were not part of it.

Elexon, a company that provides data and settlement services to the energy industry, said getting BICS up and running by next April would be “very challenging”.

Its submission said the Government had to amend laws and build new processes and systems, and much of the groundwork for that would need to be finished by early this summer.

The Treasury declined to comment on speculation that Ms Reeves would this week announce an expansion of the BICS.

data.title: Labour blames OpenAI’s cash struggles for data centre cancellation
data.description:
A Labour minister has claimed OpenAI’s “challenging” financial situation is behind the decision to halt data centre investments in the UK, rejecting claims that high energy costs are to blame.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, said that the ChatGPT developer dropped out of the project, called Stargate UK, because of its “internal financing context”.

This marks a split with OpenAI, which singled out the cost of energy when pulling the plug on an AI data centre in the North East last week.

The AI lab said it would revisit the plans, announced in September, when “the right conditions, such as regulation and the cost of energy, enable long-term infrastructure investment”.

However, Mr Narayan has now appeared to reject OpenAI’s comments.

Speaking on The Rest is Money podcast, he said there had been “no change to energy pricing, and there has been no change to the regulatory environment”.

“What has changed is, as is well publicised, OpenAI’s internal financing context has become more challenging and they have made similar calls in the United States with their programme there as well,” he said.

Britain has the highest industrial electricity prices in the developed world, although UK energy costs have fallen slightly since OpenAI unveiled plans for Stargate UK.

Data centres, and particularly AI systems, are energy-intensive, making them vulnerable to high electricity costs.

OpenAI confirmed this month that it has raised $122bn (£97bn) in new funding and the business is believed to be targeting a $1tn public float as soon as this year.

But it also burned through an estimated $14bn in 2025. This has led the company to cut back on costly projects, such as scrapping Sora, its expensive AI video app.

OpenAI, which is valued at $830bn, revealed its $500bn Stargate data centre investment plan at a White House press conference in January last year.

The project has since been beset by delays, while OpenAI has retreated from some of its other data centre ventures.

In Norway, OpenAI pulled out of another data project this week. It confirmed it would no longer rent AI computing power directly from the 100pc clean energy data site and instead lease capacity from Microsoft.

Since December, OpenAI’s international expansion efforts have been led by George Osborne, the former Conservative chancellor.

In the US, OpenAI also cancelled an expansion plan for its primary data facility in Abilene, Texas.

OpenAI declined to comment.

data.title: Giant Yorkshire gas field ‘to mine Bitcoin instead of boosting British energy’
data.description:
A giant new natural gas field in Yorkshire is to be exploited for mining Bitcoin rather than boosting Britain’s energy supplies.

Reabold Resources has been awarded a licence to carry out “gentle” fracking in the West Newton field near Hull, which is estimated to contain up to eight billion cubic metres of gas.

That would be enough to meet more than a tenth of the UK’s annual needs and makes it one of the largest onshore fields discovered in Britain, with the potential to bolster energy security for years to come.

However, Reabold instead plans to construct a small gas-fired power station on the site and use the energy produced to “mine” Bitcoin.

“A private gas supply means we can run a data centre to mine Bitcoin relatively cheaply,” said Sachin Oza, the co-chief executive of Reabold Resources, which has just been given a drilling licence by the Environment Agency.

“Initially, this would help fund the further development of the gas field and prove the concept – meaning it could become the precursor to a far larger data centre.”

The scheme has angered environmental groups opposed to new gas fields and fracking in particular.

It is also likely to raise questions in the Government after the war in Iran prompted fears of fuel shortages in the UK.

Bitcoin mining is one of the most energy-intensive of all digital activities. A power station would need to burn around 150,000 cubic metres of gas – the volume of 50 Olympic swimming pools – to generate the electricity needed to create one Bitcoin.

Reabold’s West Newton gas field is so large that it could theoretically power the creation of 50,000 Bitcoins.

Alternatively, it could help provide gas for the whole of the UK. The drilling site lies within a couple of miles of National Gas’s transmission pipeline and so could easily be connected.

Lorraine Inglis, a leader of an anti-fracking campaign group in southern England, said: “The West Newton permit exposes everything that is wrong with the UK’s current approach to energy and climate. A new onshore gas field in 2026 is fundamentally at odds with our climate commitments.

“Using that gas to power Bitcoin mining is not energy security or any genuine public benefit but the deliberate burning of fossil fuels for one of the most energy-intensive and socially questionable activities at a time of high bills and missed climate targets.”

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, imposed a ban on full-scale fracking last year. The process involves injecting high-pressure water laden with chemicals into rock to create cracks through which gas can flow.

The ban emerged after a fracking operation near Blackpool generated a series of minor earthquakes.

Despite this, the rules still allow lower-pressure fracking of the kind planned at West Newton.

Stephen Williams, the other co-chief executive at Reabold, said that while Bitcoin mining might be the first activity, it could be followed by the development of a larger data centre as more wells were drilled.

“Alternatively, we could also sell the gas to one of the adjacent industrial centres or connect it to the national gas grid,” he said.

However, the most likely outcome is that once it has permission in place to develop the field, along with planning permissions for any data centre, Reabold will sell the project to a larger firm.

It has already used a similar strategy in the North Sea, winning permits for developing the Victory field before selling the rights to Shell for £32m.

The West Newton field lies just north of Hull and the scale of the reserve first emerged when Mr Oza and Mr Williams’s team re-examined data collected by geologists in the 1940s when the wartime government was searching for domestic energy sources.

Further studies since then have been followed by preliminary drilling from a concrete pad built across farmland above.

However, the rock beneath is not as porous as the best reserves and will require “stimulation”, industry jargon for fracking.

The search for Bitcoin is becoming one of the most profitable and energy-intensive global activities, last year estimated to consume 160 terawatt hours of electricity. That’s about 50pc of the UK’s entire annual consumption.

“Owning” a Bitcoin means you have a mathematical password to a blockchain – a kind of spreadsheet – that allows you to sell your cryptocurrency to someone else.

Like all currencies, its worth depends on the belief and trust people place on it but for many users, the real value is that Bitcoin is decentralised.

This means it is held independently of any bank, is effectively invisible to governments and can be moved around the world at will.

data.title: Britain faces food shortages because of Iran war
data.description:
Britain is facing a summer of food shortages because of the Iran war. A shortage of carbon dioxide could mean chicken, pork and fizzy drinks are in short supply if the conflict continues, secret government contingency planning for a “worst-case scenario” reveals.

Senior officials from departments including No 10, the Treasury, and the Ministry of Defence rehearsed scenarios examining the potential impact of the war on British industry in an event codenamed Exercise Turnstone.

The contingency plans, seen by The Times, were based on a scenario where the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened and no deal to end the war has been reached by mid-June. Farmers were warned of potential disruption and hospitality businesses, already squeezed by Labour’s tax rises, could be hit first by a shortage of CO2.

This is because CO2 is used in the process of slaughtering pigs and chickens and making drinks fizzy. It also improves the shelf life of foods such as salads, packaged meats and cakes. 

Officials also believe a collapse in CO2 supplies could endanger lives by making dry ice used to cool blood supplies, organs and vaccines scarcer. Under a reasonable worst-case scenario, CO2 supplies would fall to just 18 per cent of current levels, and factories would be ordered to stop other manufacturing to boost supplies by up to 100 per cent.

Emergency legislation to compel factories to co-operate has been discussed, with suppliers being compensated for stopping production of their main products.

The worst-case scenario involved an important UK plant suffering a mechanical error, while high gas costs led to lower production of ammonia and fertiliser, which produces CO2 as a by-product across Europe.

Critical food shortages are not expected, but shoppers could see fewer variety of goods on supermarket shelves.

Britain is expected to be hit hard by the economic fallout from the war.

On Wednesday, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) forecast that Britain is expected to suffer the biggest hit to growth of any G7 economy.

In a triple blow for Rachel Reeves, the IMF cut its UK growth forecasts for this year and next, warned of the highest inflation in the G7 and predicted one of the smallest increases in living standards in the world.

Higher fuel prices and energy bills are forecast to cost households £480 this year, according to the Resolution Foundation, with middle-class families being hit hardest.

On Thursday, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for food shortages was under way and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course we don’t comment on leaks. But this now is out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and that he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

A government spokesman said: “We took decisive action last month to shore up the UK’s critical supplies of CO2 by temporarily restarting the Ensus bioethanol plant in Teesside and are continuing to work closely with business groups to tackle the impacts of events in the Middle East.

“Reasonable worst-case scenarios are a planning tool used by experts and are not a prediction of future events.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain has record taxes yet Labour’s mismanagement has left us broke
data.description:
The Governor of the Bank of England has warned that the world is facing a “very big energy shock” that will push up prices.

A statement of the bleeding obvious, as Basil Fawlty might say? Perhaps, but it serves to highlight just how insufficient the response has been to the economic fallout from Donald Trump’s misadventures in the Middle East.

The Government is so far out of its depth that armbands and a rubber ring may have to become compulsory items at future Cabinet meetings.

The latest iteration of the energy support scheme for industry is merely further evidence that ministers either still don’t get it or aren’t willing to provide meaningful support.

The decision to rethink the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which provides a 25pc cut in bills for so-called energy-intensive users, was supposed to demonstrate that someone in Government finally had grasped the severity of the situation facing manufacturers.

Instead, the opposite is true. Sure, expanding the breadth of it to include over 10,000 factories rather than the 7,000 first proposed is meaningful, but not if support isn’t brought forward. For reasons no one in power has been able to provide a compelling explanation for, those that qualify for relief still won’t be able to access it until April 2027.

The mind truly boggles. Without real help now, Britain risks losing some of the most critical parts of the supply chain forever, undermining our already fragile national sovereignty further and making us even more reliant on volatile foreign suppliers for materials that underpin scores of vital products.

The American owner of the UK’s last producer of a critical chemical called aniline has warned that it would close the site in Wilton, Teesside, if energy prices remained at their current levels for the next three months.

It means the amended BICS scheme, which Peter Kyle wasted little time in championing on national radio on Thursday, won’t make the slightest jot of difference to the fate of at least one major factory. The future of many more will be hanging in the balance.

The Business Secretary had the audacity to claim the changes to BICS showed Labour was “a government of action” that would “always do what’s needed” in times of global instability. Twaddle.

The only sensible conclusion is that Labour’s woeful mismanagement of the economy, under which welfare is being repeatedly prioritised over everything else including even our national security, has rendered Britain so broke that it could not afford to respond in times of genuine crisis.

This, of course, shouldn’t be the case. As the International Monetary Fund (IMF) pointed out this week, Rachel Reeves has raised taxes at the fastest pace in the world.

The Chancellor’s repeated raids on households and businesses would push the UK’s tax burden to a peacetime high of 42pc of GDP by the start of the next decade, the IMF’s figures showed. This equates to an extra £130bn of taxes since Labour took power, or a staggering £4,500 more per household.

But because of this Government’s obsession with welfare and the NHS, there is no money for energy or defence – or much else, for that matter. As Anna Leach, the chief economist at the Institute of Directors, points out, the UK is “uniquely vulnerable” to events in the Middle East because of “financing conditions, high initial starting point for inflation, uncompetitive energy costs and low fiscal space”.

It explains why the IMF expects growth in Britain this year to be slower, and inflation higher than any other major advanced economy.

The problem is spending, or rather where that spending is being directed. The intervention of Lord Robertson – a Labour grandee, former secretary-general of Nato, as well as the author of the Government’s recent strategic defence review – is telling.

Perhaps it will prove to be decisive in ensuring that some of the ever-expanding welfare budget is redirected to other departments. When serious figures like Robertson are starting to openly question the benefits bill, it is surely time for a complete rethink of where resources are being channelled. As he points out, Britain’s welfare budget is now five times the amount we spend on defence at a time when national security has never been more vital.

Perhaps naive ministers have allowed the latest GDP figures to fool them into thinking the economy could weather the incoming storm.

No doubt February’s numbers showing a 0.5pc rise in GDP are a pleasant surprise, but any growth had almost certainly already been extinguished by events in the Middle East – Kyle himself acknowledged this.

It is why the failure to provide meaningful and immediate support for heavy industry makes no sense. Left to fend for themselves in the face of even higher energy and raw material costs than they were already grappling with thanks to the war in Ukraine, and endless government-imposed green levies, manufacturers will be forced to curtail investment, cut back on hiring, and possibly even reduce output. Under those circumstances, growth becomes little more than an aspiration.

Yet, at the same time, ministers are drawing up plans to invoke emergency legislation to force many of those very same factories to double CO2 production to avoid shortages that will ripple through the economy. Forget a fizzy drinks crisis, a CO2 scarcity could quickly become a threat to life by affecting supplies of vaccines, blood and vital medicines.

Given this, you would think ministers would be bending over backwards to provide help. Instead, the best they could come up with was a rehashed scheme that still wouldn’t be available for another year – proof that the disconnect at the top has reached epic new proportions.

On Thursday, Sir Keir Starmer told tech bosses hauled into No 10 to discuss child safety: “The status quo simply cannot be allowed to stand.” With local elections around the corner, the same could be said of Labour’s time in power.

data.title: London’s housing crisis deepens as plans for 42,000 new homes scrapped
data.description:
Developers have mothballed at least 42,000 new London homes in a major blow to Sir Sadiq Khan’s hopes of ending the capital’s housebuilding crisis, according to new analysis.

At least 30,000 homes that had planning consent and a further 12,000 that had been allocated in local authority plans have been scrapped and switched to other uses, according to exclusive figures from Savills.

Katy Warrick, head of London residential development research at the property group, said anecdotal evidence suggested that the actual number could be as high as 50,000 homes.

The housing sites have largely been mothballed in the last three years, as residential developers have been struck by soaring regulations, high interest rates and low buyer demand.

Ms Warrick said these sites were now being used for warehouses, data centres, self-storage units, industrial sites and hotels, rather than homes.

It marks a major setback for the Mayor of London, who is grappling with a critical housing crisis in the capital and is failing to meet his homes targets by a significant margin.

The number of abandoned plans will be particularly concerning considering just how few homes received planning consent in London.

Slightly more than 20,000 homes got sign-off last year, a number that was already just a fraction of the 88,000 homes that London is supposed to build each year in line with Labour’s housing targets.

It follows claims that private housebuilding in the capital has been killed “stone-dead” by policies in recent years.

Lord Bailey of Paddington, a former Tory mayoral candidate, released figures last year showing London was building the fewest homes of any English region per capita.

Experts have claimed that new building safety regulations introduced in the wake of the tragic Grenfell fire are having a disproportionate impact in London, where a much higher share of new-build homes are in high-rise blocks.

High mortgage rates are also hammering buyer demand in the capital, making it less attractive to build new homes.

Key housing sites that have been lost include the Honeymonster Factory in Southall, which had planning permission for nearly 2,000 homes and is now owned by a developer that is instead planning to build a data centre.

John Lewis announced in February that it was closing down its housebuilding business, scrapping plans to build 1,000 homes across three sites, two of which were in London and one in the commuter belt, citing cost increases.

London-focused Berkeley Group announced it was halting all new land purchases earlier this month because of “unprecedented” increases in costs and regulation, as well as low buyer demand.

Ms Warrick said: “This has been slowly building over the last three or four years as the viability of residential development has been in a long, painful decline.

“When you bear in mind what the cost is and how complicated residential development is, it’s not worth it.”

Residential development was always a “slam-dunk”, best-value approach for developers before the pandemic, Ms Warrick said.

Since then, however, build costs, regulations, and interest rates have all soared. On top of this, the Government’s Help to Buy equity loan, which allowed buyers to purchase new build homes with 20pc government-backed equity loans that were interest-free for the first five years, disappeared in 2023.

Housing developers have claimed that these changes mean that the sums to build homes simply do not add up. Neil Jefferson, the chief executive of the Home Builders Federation (HBF), an industry group, said: “Viability is really becoming an issue.”

A spokesman for the Mayor of London said new emergency measures confirmed last month, including temporary relief from the community infrastructure levy, would ramp up building.

They said: “By reducing the demands on developers in the short-term, the measures will result in more homes including affordable homes being provided to those who need them most.”

data.title: Netflix co-founder to step down after spearheading streaming revolution
data.description:
The businessman who turned Netflix from a DVD delivery service into the world’s biggest streaming giant has announced plans to step down as chairman.

Reed Hastings, who co-founded Netflix alongside Marc Randolph in 1997, said on Thursday that he would leave the US media company after three decades in June.

It brings an end to 29 years at Netflix for Mr Hastings, who revolutionised TV entertainment and turned the US company into a $455bn media behemoth.

He is most credited with being the driving force behind Netflix’s pivot from delivering DVDs in red envelopes to becoming a worldwide streamer with original content.

Announcing his departure alongside Netflix’s latest results, which recorded revenue growth of 16pc to $12.3bn, Mr Hastings said: “Netflix changed my life in so many ways, and my all‑time favourite memory was January 2016, when we enabled nearly the entire planet to enjoy our service.”

Shares in Netflix fell by 9.3pc in the wake of the announcement.

Long-time Democrat donor

The billionaire businessman said he now planned to focus on “other pursuits”, potentially including the Utah ski resort that he bought in 2023.

He previously announced plans to turn half of the mountain into a members-only ski club, with accompanying residential developments.

Mr Hastings, who has previously served on the boards of Microsoft and Facebook, is also a long-time Democrat donor. He has given more than $20m to Democratic causes in recent years, including to Joe Biden’s 2020 and 2024 presidential campaigns.

However, in 2024, he told the New York Times that Mr Biden should withdraw from the race and “allow a vigorous democratic leader” to stand against Donald Trump.

Though generally viewed as a Silicon Valley liberal, he has more recently voiced support for Mr Trump’s $100,000 visa fee for foreign workers, calling it “a great solution”.

During his 24-year stint as chief executive between 1999 and 2023, Mr Hastings transformed Netflix into the most valuable entertainment company in the world, with an estimated audience approaching one billion.

He had previously boasted that he considered sleep to be the streaming platform’s biggest rival, telling an audience in Los Angeles in 2017: “We’re competing with sleep, on the margin. And so it’s a very large pool of time.”

Mr Hastings has been gradually winding down his responsibilities at Netflix over recent years. He appointed Ted Sarandos as his co-chief executive in 2020 before choosing Greg Peters to replace him in the joint leadership role in 2023.

Boosted by US price rises

His departure was announced alongside Netflix’s first set of results since the streaming giant backed out of a blockbuster bidding war to buy Warner Bros.

Netflix had struck an $83bn deal to buy the Hollywood studio, which is home to the Harry Potter film series and HBO TV shows, but was usurped when rival bidder Paramount tabled a knockout $111bn offer.

Netflix bosses have argued that they took a disciplined approach to the acquisition, insisting that Warner Bros was a “nice to have” at the right price.

Shares in the streaming service had risen almost 30pc since it backed out of the deal, which had not been viewed positively by investors. The company has also cashed in a $2.8bn break fee from Paramount, giving it extra firepower to fund new shows.

Netflix has also been boosted by recent price rises in the US – the second time it has increased prices in the last year.

The company, which no longer discloses subscriber numbers, has been building its cheaper, advertising-supported tier in an effort to hold on to viewers in the face of growing cost-of-living pressures.

It has also been pushing further into live sports as the streaming service seeks to generate more revenue from ads and keep audiences on its platform.

data.title: Miliband to wipe out energy debt of poorest households in Iran bailout
data.description:
Poor households are in line to have their debts to energy suppliers paid down by taxpayers under plans being considered by Ed Miliband.

The Energy Secretary is in talks to soften the blow of the Iran conflict on family fuel bills with public funds, industry sources said.

The move is one option on the table as the Government grapples with ways to provide targeted support and avoid the costly blanket subsidies deployed in the Ukraine crisis.

Ofgem, the energy regulator, has already prepared a debt relief scheme that will forgive around £500m in debt accumulated by some 195,000 families during the 2022 energy crisis.

But the launch of that scheme has now been temporarily delayed while ministers consider expanding it to account for the conflict in the Middle East, The Telegraph understands.

It is not clear how such an expansion would be paid for.

Paying for debt relief

The cost of the existing £500m scheme is being spread across all households, with the average bill-payer expected to see £5 added to their bills by 2027-28 to fund the debt forgiveness.

Piling any further costs onto households is likely to be controversial, particularly given Labour’s pledge to cut bills by £300 a year by 2030.

Another way to pay for the debt scheme could be an increase in windfall taxes on renewable electricity generators, which Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, is expected to announce this week.

A spokesman for Ofgem declined to comment on Monday due to “purdah” rules ahead of May’s local elections.

There is growing concern across the energy industry about surging household debts, which officially reached £4.6bn last year but is expected to hit £7bn by the end of 2026 – more than triple 2022’s total.

Persistently high energy costs are largely to blame for the increase in debts, according to experts, although there has also been an uptick in people choosing not to pay.

The increase is problematic not only for those indebted to energy suppliers, but also for other households who have to pay an average surcharge of £50 a year to cover the overall shortfall.

Now, with the Middle East crisis sending fuel costs surging higher, there is concern that the debt mountain will grow larger as poorer families struggle to make ends meet.

Bills fell for millions of people this month when Ofgem’s energy price cap dropped from £1,758 to £1,641 a year.

But it is then expected to rise again to about £1,837 per year in July, following surges in the gas price, according to analysts at the consultancy Cornwall Insight.

This has prompted calls for a support package for households, although ministers have so far stressed that it will only be targeted at the poorest.

Ms Reeves has also warned against overreacting with “knee-jerk responses” that could worsen the Government’s finances in the long run.

‘Ready to go’

An industry source said the ease of expanding the existing debt relief scheme was that it was “ready to go,” already targeted at the vulnerable and could simply be adapted.

Some energy companies are nervous about the proposals for further debt relief amid concerns it will create a “moral hazard” and encourage people not to pay, while others are understood to be lobbying in support.

A person familiar with the scheme stressed that households would only be granted relief if they were engaging with their supplier and had agreed to a repayment plan.

Ofgem is also considering changes that would allow energy suppliers to cut off households who fail to register themselves within a fortnight of moving into a property.

Currently, when new tenants move in, accounts are switched to an anonymous “occupier” status and bills build up until the new homeowners contact the supplier.

The regulator said the changes aimed to prevent people from running up unpaid debts when they move house, as part of efforts to tackle bill-dodgers.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “Tackling the affordability crisis is our number one priority. 

“Thanks to the Government’s action at the Budget, the energy price cap has fallen by 7pc in April, protecting households with lower bills until the end of June.

“We are working urgently with Ofgem to drive debt out of the system, including on its proposals for a potential debt relief scheme, and to deliver an energy market that puts people first and lowers bills for everyone.”

data.title: The accident waiting to happen to Britain’s trillion-pound pensions industry
data.description:
Success in public policy has been so thin on the ground in recent years that it is hard to point to a single example of it. But to my mind there is at least one exception: the idea of pensions “auto-enrolment”.

This came into force in 2012 after a lengthy Pensions Commission inquiry into shortfalls in retirement savings under the chairmanship of Lord Adair Turner, a former McKinsey high-flyer and director-general of the Confederation of British Industry.

The key recommendation was that employees would automatically be required to funnel a bare minimum of their earnings – matched by the employer – into a pension pot unless they deliberately opted out.

Even in conjunction with a much enhanced state pension, Turner’s initiative has come nowhere near solving the problem of deficient income in retirement.

But though in most cases the sums involved will fall a long way short of buying an annual cruise in the Caribbean, auto-enrolment has ensured that many more workers do put away at least something for later.

At the last count, the default fund for these savings – the state-backed National Employment Savings Trust (Nest) – had around £50bn under management.

This is expected to swell to at least £100bn by the end of the decade, a tidy sum which goes some way to addressing a long-standing structural weakness at the heart of the UK economy – that as a nation we save too little, but borrow and spend too much.

Plainly, today’s arrangements are not nearly as good as the system of private sector final-salary pension provision that was once the norm, but that boat sailed a long time ago, and it’s not coming back. Auto-enrolment does at least go some way to filling the gap.

Unfortunately, this is where the plaudits end, for there is no mess quite so great as the various rules, regulations, and government interventions that surround pensions savings.

The propensity of ministers to interfere shows no sign of abating, and indeed has shifted up a gear or two under the current Chancellor, Rachel Reeves. Wherever there’s a pot of other people’s money to go after, you can bet your boots the Treasury has got its eyes firmly set on it.

Recent interventions come in three forms. First, the Government has further undermined some of the tax incentives for pension savings by capping National Insurance relief on salary sacrifice arrangements.

Second, it is making pension pots subject to inheritance tax where previously they were exempt.

And third, it is trying to force pension funds to invest in private, British assets such as infrastructure, private credit and private equity.

I don’t altogether disagree with the patriotic intent of “mandating” pension fund investment for the supposed national interest.

It is obviously desirable that at least some of Britain’s pool of domestic savings gets ploughed back into UK productive assets.

This used to be the case when the big final-salary pension schemes ruled the land, but globalisation and government liability matching regulation destroyed all that, and in today’s world it is much more difficult to argue that pension money should be confined in the way proposed under legislation currently stuck in the House of Lords.

Imagine if our pension pots had been invested exclusively in the FTSE All-Share, UK gilts, or worse still, the grand folly of HS2.

You’d have missed out on one of the greatest bull markets of all time – America’s S&P 500. Sadly, much of today’s growth lies beyond these shores. It is wrong in principle to deny savers the opportunity to invest in it.

Ministers cannot magic up US levels of advancement simply by forcing savers to invest their money in UK assets. This is in any case putting the cart before the horse.

Britain should concern itself first with building an economy where people actually want to invest. Financial coercion is no substitute for the hard yards of wider economic reform.

Even in Britain, there really is no shortage of capital for worthwhile commercial investment. You scarcely need to mandate pension funds to provide it.

Yet where policy actively conspires to drive entrepreneurs and wealth away, and where it further forces savers to do what they wouldn’t voluntarily do, returns are bound to be disappointing.

But who needs coercion when you’ve got Nest? According to reports, trustees are lining up plans to invest up to 30pc of funds under management in private markets. The legislation requires only 10pc, with just half of that earmarked for UK assets.

Please don’t say Nest is under any pressure to do this from its masters in Downing Street. No sir, this is an entirely independent, arm’s-length decision.

All I can say is I’m glad not to be making a similar investment decision and, for that matter, that I am not invested in Nest. The chances of things going wrong, disadvantaging Nest’s 13 million individual pension pots, are high.

Don’t take it from me. Just ask the Pensions Regulator, which has – embarrassingly for ministers – written to 58 schemes warning of potentially significant costs from undue exposure to private markets.

The regulator’s point is that when assets are private, they are both hard to value and even harder to sell when liquidation is required.

“Exiting private markets under pressure is less an orderly divestment and more a negotiated surrender of value,” Nikesh Patel, of investment management firm Van Lanschot Kempen, told the Financial Times.

Indeed so, as well publicised problems in US private credit markets have recently highlighted.

This is a world of “mark to fantasy”, as opposed to the realism of “mark to market”.

Surrounded by a veritable army of sponging, fee-hungry consultants, many trustees have no idea what they are doing and are being sold a pup.

It’s bad enough for savers when money managers do it voluntarily; just imagine the missteps when they are forced to do it under pressure from the Government in order to meet quotas.

The parallel is with the sort of misallocation of resources once seen at the Department for International Development when approaching the end of the financial year having underspent the budget. On a spend-it-or-lose-it basis, almost any Tom, Dick or Harry would be seen as worthy of a handout.

The investment bankers must be positively drooling at the prospect. According to Calvera’s well-known line in the Magnificent Seven: “If God didn’t want them sheared, he would not have made them sheep.”

I don’t want to be rude about a generally well-intentioned bunch, but the same might be said of many pension fund trustees. It’s an accident waiting to happen if ever there was one.

It’s not their fault. They are mere victims of a political mindset that believes economic growth can be conjured up by simply telling people how best to invest their money. As they are about to find out, that’s not how it works.
