data.title: Tories promise to abolish 24-hour bus lanes
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to abolish 24-hour bus lanes to ease traffic congestion.

The party is launching their six-point plan for motorists before the local elections on May 7 with a promise of “bus lanes that make sense”.

Kemi Badenoch, the Tory leader, said they would restrict the operating hours of bus lanes to allow cars to use them more frequently.

Mrs Badenoch said Labour wanted “to make drivers’ lives as difficult as possible”. In recent years, the number of bus lanes operating 24 hours a day, seven days a week, has soared in cities across the Midlands and Merseyside.

In London, more than 50 miles of bus lanes became a 24-hour operation in 2021 under Heidi Alexander, the former deputy mayor for transport and now the transport secretary.

Under Tory plans, all 24/7 bus lanes will be scrapped unless a government minister provides specific written permission for one to remain open.

Conservative sources pointed to a Tory council in West Northamptonshire scrapping a 24-hour bus lane, making it only operational between 7.30am and 9.30am.

Manchester city council issued the highest number of fines for bus lane violations, at more than 500,000 between 2023 and 2025.

According to data from used-car firm Cinch, Bristol city council came second at 344,000 fines, followed by Sheffield city council at 275,360.

The implementation and operation of bus lanes is down to individual councils to decide what is best for their area.

Government guidance says bus lanes “should be provided only when they are needed” and operate “only when buses are running” or when traffic is sufficiently busy to cause delays.

It adds that local authorities should make sure that if a bus lane does operate at quieter times of the day, there is a strong case for it and the effect on other users is “mitigated”.

The Tories’ six-point plan for motorists also includes a promise to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Tory councils will review all 20mph measures, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The Conservatives have also promised to scrap the rise in fuel duty planned by the Chancellor for September, and unroll a “national pothole patrol” to fix Britain’s roads.

The party also said it would recruit more driving test examiners and increase training capacity to reduce the backlog, which has risen by more than three weeks under Labour.

A Labour spokesman said: “Nobody should let the Tories behind the wheel again. After 14 years in charge, they left our roads riddled with potholes and the driving test system in chaos, with waiting times spiralling on their watch.”

data.title: Bank of England urges lenders to strengthen defences against AI
data.description:
The Bank of England has told City firms to bolster their cyber defences against the threat of new artificial intelligence-powered hacking bots.

Banks, insurers and payments firms were summoned to a meeting on Wednesday to discuss the cybersecurity risks posed by “frontier AI models”, which include Anthropic’s new Mythos bot that was deemed too dangerous to release to the public.

At the meeting, which was held by the Bank of England’s Cross Market Operational Resilience Group (CMORG), City firms were urged to strengthen their cybersecurity.

This could be done by using AI to fix vulnerabilities and automate responses to any threats, CMORG said, adding that it planned to hold another meeting in May with a broader range of companies.

CMORG was set up in 2019 to deal with systemic threats faced by Britain’s finance industry.

It comes after revelations about Anthropic’s new Mythos model prompted panic among cybersecurity experts, who raised concerns that the AI tool could wreak havoc if it ends up in the wrong hands.

Anthropic unveiled the Mythos model earlier this month but has held back from releasing it publicly.

The AI developer has warned Mythos’s hacking abilities “surpass all but the most skilled humans at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities”, and said the model threatens to “reshape cybersecurity”.

The warning led to Wall Street banks being summoned to a crisis meeting this month by Scott Bessent, the US treasury secretary, and Jerome Powell, the chairman of the Federal Reserve.

MI5 is also racing to bolster the defences of Britain’s critical infrastructure, urging water, energy and communications companies to be aware of Mythos’s capabilities.

An evaluation of Mythos by the Artificial Intelligence Security Institute, a UK-funded research lab, found that the model was able to autonomously carry out advanced cyberattacks that would take a human hacker days.

In its report, the research lab added that future AI models “will be more capable still”, as it urged companies to boost their cyber defences.

Banks and technology companies including Amazon, Apple and JP Morgan have been given access to Anthropic’s powerful AI tool to let them stress-test their cyber defences, through a scheme called Project Glasswing.

However, on Tuesday, Anthropic revealed it was investigating claims that some unauthorised people had gained access to the Mythos tool through the scheme.

It said it did not have evidence the tool had been accessed beyond the “vendor environment”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BP hit by investor rebellion after net zero retreat
data.description:
BP has been hit by a major investor rebellion after watering down its net zero plans to instead focus on oil and gas.

Almost a fifth of investors voted against the re-election of Albert Manifold, the company’s new chairman, at the business’s annual general meeting (AGM) on Thursday.

Mr Manifold, who became chairman in September, was re-elected with just 81.77pc of voting shareholders’ backing. Most executives at major public companies are elected with the backing of more than 95pc of investors.

Legal & General, one of Europe’s biggest asset managers, had said before the AGM that it would vote against Mr Manifold’s re-election, while Glass Lewis, a proxy adviser, recommended investors do the same.

Shareholders also rejected BP’s attempts to move to online AGMs and scrap the publication of company-specific climate reports.

Investors have been angered by the company’s decision to retreat from net zero plans drawn up in 2020, water down climate reporting and refocus wholeheartedly on oil and gas.

A former construction materials chief, one of Mr Manifold’s first moves was to appoint Meg O’Neill, a seasoned oil executive, to lead the company.

He also refused to bring a shareholder climate resolution to a vote and provoked controversy by planning to end BP’s climate impact reporting commitments.

Thursday’s stormy meeting saw a raft of shareholder challenges to executives over their plans, with one persistent questioner finally removed by BP’s security guards.

A spokesman for Rathbones said the wealth manager had bought 43 million shares in BP based on the company’s net zero commitments and demanded: “safeguards to provide assurance to shareholders that their rights ... will be protected.”

Addressing shareholders at the gathering in Sunbury-on-Thames, Surrey, Mr Manifold said: “BP’s performance in recent years has not been where you – or we – want it to be. The job is to ensure BP delivers the sustainable, long-term returns you expect from us.

“We need to be a simpler, stronger, more valuable company. In service of that, we have made decisive changes to BP’s strategy and to the leadership.”

Ms O’Neill, who joined BP this month from Australian oil firm Woodside Energy, pledged more change in pursuit of bigger profits.

She said: “BP is a great company. It can be even greater. Now, we need to accelerate the progress we have made so far. We need to strengthen the balance sheet and be rigorous in our spending and investment. Our next chapter will be as a simpler, stronger, more valuable company.”

Ms O’Neill was re-elected with far more support, winning the backing of 96.76pc of shareholders who voted.

Ms O’Neill is the first woman to take on the top job at the 117-year-old company. However, she is also BP’s third chief executive in under five years.

Gavin Palmer, who described himself as a BP investor and investment analyst, was removed from the meeting after criticising the company, demanding: “How did you get through so many chief executives?”

A key AGM battle was over the board’s proposal to repeal its 2019 pledge to report detailed climate-related information, including its management of planet-heating emissions, low-carbon investments, public policy positions and how executive pay is linked to progress.

The company wanted to ditch these “legacy” obligations in favour of wider industry reporting guidelines. Campaigners argued this would let BP exclude much of the detail of its environmental impacts.

Investors rejected BP’s attempt to scrap the reporting requirements.

A separate but related battle was over the BP board’s rejection of a shareholder resolution from the Dutch activist group Follow This. It wanted BP to publish plans for how it would maintain shareholder value as fossil-fuel demand falls with the transition to clean energy.

A similar resolution has been accepted at Shell and will go to a shareholder vote at its meeting next month.

Mark van Baal, the chief executive of Follow This, accused Mr Manifold of “deepening BP’s governance crisis”.

Mr Manifold claimed the proposal was legally invalid. BP has said the board had decided, having taken legal advice, that the proposed resolution “did not conform to legal requirements” and would be ineffective if it were to pass.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The businesses that won’t let you pay by card
data.description:
Do you rely on cash in your everyday life? Contact us at money@telegraph.co.uk*

When Hugh Corcoran opened his business in 2024, he decided to run it the old-fashioned way.

The Yellow Bittern, a restaurant and bookshop in north London, has a quaint hand-painted sign outside and a ring-for-entry doorbell. Reservations can only be taken by phone or in person – and customers can only pay with cash.

“We chose not to install a payment system or a credit card machine because we wanted to keep things as simple as possible,” Corcoran, 36, says.

“We are a very small restaurant (20 covers) and a very small team (three people) so we did not want anything more than what we see as necessary to the business – that being good wines in the cellar, good food in the kitchen and a table to sit at.”

As Britain moves towards becoming a cashless society, The Yellow Bittern is one of a few businesses bucking the trend.

According to cash machine network Link, the number of retailers on Britain’s high streets going cashless has risen by 14pc in the last year with 23pc now eschewing cash completely.

Many cite security concerns as a factor in their decision, as well as a lack of demand for cash payments from customers.

For those who tend to favour cash, including older and more vulnerable people, this change is keenly felt.

For Belfast-born Corcoran, who lived in France for 10 years before moving to London, the lack of cash acceptance in the capital came as a surprise.

Although some see it as a minor inconvenience, the majority of The Yellow Bittern’s customers know to bring cash now. Others are able to withdraw it from nearby cashpoints.

The only issue comes occasionally when customers only have a smartphone rather than a physical card.

“The idea of being so reliant on a smartphone is completely crazy to me,” says Corcoran. “What if you misplace your telephone or your battery dies?”

For Corcoran, using cash is the most straightforward option for his small business: “We keep a log of accounts by hand. I sell food and wine and receive cash payment for it, like my father did when he fixed cars in his garage. It’s very uncomplicated.”

The Red Brick Café in Bridport, Dorset, has been in business for 17 years and has always operated a “cash-only” policy.

“The main reason for running the café in this way is to support local farmers, growers and producers,” says Hayley Mason, a co-owner.

“Farmers can’t always get to the bank, especially with fewer local branches open. We also save on the costs of having a card machine and the charges that entails,” says the 45-year-old.

According to a House of Commons report from March, 6,700 bank or building society branches have closed their doors since January 2015, some 68pc. The number of cash machines has also fallen by 40pc within the same period.

Accepting card payments can also rack up significant costs, especially for small business owners.

Card readers or point-of-sale terminals typically charge somewhere between 1.5pc and 3.4pc per transaction. Businesses may also have to pay a rental fee for their terminal or may be subject to minimum charges.

The community is supportive of The Red Brick Café’s policy overall, says Mason. If a customer doesn’t have cash, they can order and sit down while someone else in the group goes to the cashpoint just a couple of minutes’ walk away.

“Children learn from it too by saving money and physically counting to see if they can afford something,” she says. “And elderly people sometimes forget their Pin, so often prefer cash.”

The café did briefly have a card machine during the Covid pandemic in order to stay open for customers during a difficult time.

“As soon as things settled down after the pandemic though, we got rid of the card reader,” Mason says. “If we don’t start making a stand and use our cash, we might lose it. I don’t want to be part of this system where everything is ‘tap tap tap’.”

Shops can currently accept whatever form of payment they want but campaigners are pushing for new laws similar to those in other countries which force retailers to accept cash.

Clare Beaumont, 51, runs Beaumont grocers in Nunhead, south London. For her, cash is king – although she will reluctantly accept card payments when necessary.

The main motivation for her is trying to alleviate the card-machine fees she has to pay every month.

“I’m a small business. Some weeks we may make a profit, other weeks we’re just paying the overheads,” she says.

“But it’s also a fear of cash being eliminated. Online payments are encouraged so much and I don’t want cash to disappear.”

She adds that a couple of places down the road from her shop have gone cashless and the elderly customers won’t use them any more.

“I want to encourage cash and encourage the younger ones to start using it again,” she says.

While 77pc of high street retailers still accept cash, according to Link, the decline in cash is bad news for those who find it more convenient or who struggle to use other payment methods.

Research by The Payments Association in 2025 found 19pc of people from lower socio-economic groups preferred to use cash, compared with 12pc of those from higher groups.

It also found that older people tended to favour cash more than the young. It noted that the older someone is, the more regularly they use cash, with 72pc of people aged 55 or older using cash at least once a month.

“Cash may not be king any more for the majority of people but people on the lowest incomes are clearly more reliant on cash,” says Nick Quin, of Link.

“Businesses that opt to go cashless cite customer demand as one reason for this choice but they may also be concerned about security, fraud and the ability to deposit their cash.”

For the Inns of Court pub in Wakefield, West Yorkshire, there is a simple reason why it has opted to stay cash-only for the last 22 years – to protect their customers.

“Cards get you into debt. I don’t want to rob people, I disagree with it,” says Trish Thornton, 77, who helps run the pub for Dave Burns, 78, the proprietor.

“If you can’t afford to come out and pay for your beer in cash, you can’t afford to pay for it. People want to have a drink and if they’re just tapping their card, they end up with no money at the end of the month.”

Customers are generally supportive of the policy, although Thornton has noticed a generational divide in their attitude.

“It’s usually the young ones who have an issue – the older customers always have cash.”

data.title: Drivers ‘owed hundreds of pounds more’ in motor finance scandal
data.description:
Drivers who were mis-sold car finance should be getting hundreds of pounds more in compensation, a consumer group has claimed, as it prepares to take the City watchdog to court because of its £9.1bn redress scheme.

Consumer Voice accused the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) of siding with lenders in how its compensation scheme was designed for millions of drivers who were mis-sold car finance.

The group has notified the City watchdog that it is planning to launch a legal challenge because of the programme, which will cover some 12.1 million car loan deals.

Compensation is being made to drivers who were sold car finance agreements in which dealers received a fee from lenders based on the interest rate charged on the loans.

The FCA, which banned these agreements in 2021, argued that these fees were rarely disclosed to customers and provided an incentive for dealers to charge higher interest rates than necessary.

The watchdog laid out details of its compensation scheme last month, saying millions of drivers who had signed up for these deals as long ago as 2007 would receive £830 each.

However, Alex Neill, co-founder of Consumer Voice, said: “The FCA has designed a scheme that leaves ordinary motorists hundreds of pounds per claim out of pocket. That cannot be left unchallenged.

“Consumers have been let down by the lenders who mis-sold them car finance. They should not be let down again by the regulator that is meant to protect them. The FCA must fix the scheme and deliver the fair redress that millions of UK motorists are owed.”

Consumer Voice is understood to believe that drivers are missing out on between £200 and £300 per claim. However, this could rise to between £800 and £900 in some cases.

It would mark the first time a consumer-focused group has challenged the regulator because of a compensation scheme in UK courts. It would also be the first legal challenge to the motor finance scheme.

If successful, the legal action could force lenders to carve out billions of pounds more for compensation to drivers.

It comes after years when the motor finance scandal has weighed on lenders, hitting the share price of several Britain’s biggest banks and forcing them to book billions of pounds worth of provisions.

FCA ‘underestimates the true harm suffered’

Consumer Voice helps law firms represent claimants in group actions to promote their claims. The group has partnered with law firm Courmacs Legal to file the challenge.

Courmacs Legal is separately filing a legal challenge against Lloyds Banking Group on behalf of customers, seeking damages for historic car-leasing agreements. Lloyds has declined to comment on the action.

Consumer Voice said it believed that the FCA had misapplied an earlier Supreme Court ruling on car finance, which it claimed meant that the vast majority of complaints would not receive full redress.

The group said: “Most consumers will receive redress based on a complex ‘hybrid’ redress calculation that is fundamentally flawed and significantly underestimates the true harm suffered.”

An FCA spokesman said: “Our scheme is the quickest, fairest way to compensate consumers. It seems contradictory that organisations claiming to represent consumers would seek to delay payouts for millions of people.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘Hairdryer used to trick weather sensor’ to win $34,000 bet
data.description:
French police are investigating suspicions that a hairdryer may have been used to tamper with official weather readings to make thousands of dollars in Polymarket bets.

Temperature readings at Paris’s Charles de Gaulle airport have unexpectedly spiked twice in the last month, reaching levels much higher than expected.

On both days, gamblers on Polymarket, the world’s biggest prediction market, appear to have made huge sums by betting on unlikely weather patterns. The site relied on readings from the Charles de Gaulle temperature sensor.

Météo-France, the country’s official weather agency, said it had complained to police after noticing a change to one of its temperature sensors.

“In view of physical findings on one of our instruments and the analysis of sensor data, Météo-France was indeed led to file a complaint for alteration of the operation of an automated data processing system with the Air Transport Gendarmerie Brigade of Roissy,” a spokesman said.

“As the investigation is ongoing, we do not wish to make any further comments in order to ensure the proper conduct of the procedure.”

On April 6 and April 15, temperatures at the airport’s weather sensor unexpectedly spiked late in the day, with the peak temperature reading significantly higher than forecast.

On April 6, the sensor recorded a peak temperature of 21C at 7pm and on April 15, the peak temperature of 22C was reached at 9:30pm.

On both days, the peak temperature had hovered around 18C during the day.

Polymarket, where people can use cryptocurrency to bet on thousands of different markets from politics to war, ran a daily market on the peak temperature in Paris using the Charles de Gaulle airport weather station.

On both days, the markets put the chance of a 21C or 22C day at less than 1pc, meaning people who bet on the outcome earlier in the day would have made a substantial return on their initial stakes.

On April 15, one trader made $21,000 (£15,600) betting that the maximum temperature would not be 18 degrees, data from Polymarket show. A temperature of 18C was seen as a 99.6pc probability before the temperature spiked later in the day.

Successful bets on the temperature reportedly netted around $34,000.

While it is unclear how the temperature might have been manipulated, one possibility shared on weather forums was that a battery-powered hairdryer could have been used, according to Le Monde newspaper.

The station is located on a public road, making it easy to access.

The French Air Transport Gendarmerie and Polymarket were contacted for comment.

There are no indications so far that the successful punters have had to return their winnings. However, the data source for Paris’s hottest temperature has since moved to a sensor at the smaller Paris-Le Bourget airport.
data.author: The telegraph
